adams-v-sch-bd-of-st-johns-cnty-57-f-4th.title: Adams v. Sch. Bd. of St. Johns Cnty.
adams-v-sch-bd-of-st-johns-cnty-57-f-4th.summary: Drew Adams, a transgender male student, challenged a school-board policy requiring students to use bathrooms corresponding to biological sex rather than gender identity. After the district court ruled for Adams, the Eleventh Circuit sitting en banc took up whether that policy violated Title IX or the Equal Protection Clause. The court answered no on both fronts and reversed. It held that separating school bathrooms by biological sex fit the governing statutory and constitutional framework and that the board?s policy did not amount to unlawful sex discrimination. The reasoning emphasized the text of Title IX, the accepted legality of sex-separated school facilities, and the court?s view that the policy drew lines based on biological distinctions the law still recognizes in that setting.

all-american-quality-foods-inc-v-britten.title: All American Quality Foods, Inc. v. Britten
all-american-quality-foods-inc-v-britten.summary: Bernice Britten sued a grocery store after falling while shopping, but she admitted she never saw what caused her fall and could identify no liquid or defect except for an unidentified bystander's comment about water. The trial court denied summary judgment, and All American Quality Foods took an interlocutory appeal. The issues were whether Britten had evidence that a dangerous condition actually caused her fall and whether the store had superior knowledge of that condition. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed. It held that speculation about water on the floor, especially when Britten could not identify the cause herself and the video evidence showed employees later reacting to an ice pack leak, was not enough to create a triable causation issue. Without competent evidence tying the fall to a premises defect known to the store, the negligence claim could not survive summary judgment.

allred-v-progressive-cty-mut-ins-co-374-.title: Allred v. Progressive Cty. Mut. Ins. Co.
allred-v-progressive-cty-mut-ins-co-374-.summary: Stephanie Allred sought uninsured-motorist benefits under her parents' automobile policy after she was struck by a vehicle in Atlanta, but Progressive argued that she was excluded from coverage because she no longer qualified as a resident of their household. The trial court granted summary judgment to the insurer, and Allred appealed. The issue was whether the undisputed evidence established, as a matter of law, that Allred's domicile and residence had changed so she fell outside the policy. The Court of Appeals of Georgia said no and reversed. It held that disputes about living arrangements, household membership, and intent to remain or separate are usually fact-intensive questions for a jury rather than matters suitable for summary adjudication. Because the record turned on contested evidence about the family's actual residential relationship, Progressive was not entitled to judgment as a matter of law.

arnold-v-liggins-368-ga-app-544.title: Arnold v. Liggins
arnold-v-liggins-368-ga-app-544.summary: After Scott Arnold rear-ended Joshua Liggins, Arnold admitted fault but contested causation and damages, and a jury returned a $70,000 verdict plus a later award of litigation expenses under OCGA 9-11-68. Arnold appealed, arguing that the trial court mishandled evidence about Liggins' prior accident, accepted an improper medical-bill exhibit, let closing argument exceed the pretrial order, and wrongly awarded statutory fees. The Court of Appeals of Georgia rejected Arnold's trial-evidence complaints but reversed the fee award. It held that the settlement-offer provisions of OCGA 9-11-68 were not satisfied because Liggins failed to comply with the statute's service requirements even though he recovered more than his offer amount. The judgment was therefore affirmed as to the verdict and costs but reversed as to attorney fees and litigation expenses.

atlanta-area-council-inc-v-patel.title: Atlanta Area Council, Inc. v. Patel
atlanta-area-council-inc-v-patel.summary: A Boy Scouts volunteer caused a car crash while driving her own vehicle to a restaurant meeting about Cub Scout day-camp planning, and the injured driver sought to hold the Atlanta Area Council vicariously liable. After the trial court denied summary judgment, the Council pursued an interlocutory appeal. The issue was whether the Council could be liable under respondeat superior when the volunteer was traveling to the meeting rather than actively conducting the organization's business at the time of the collision. The Court of Appeals of Georgia held that it could not and reversed. Assuming without deciding that an employment-type relationship existed, the court applied Georgia's going-and-coming rule and concluded that the volunteer was commuting to the meeting, driving her own car, and talking on a personal call when the accident happened. Because the undisputed evidence showed she was not acting within the scope of the Council's business at that moment, vicarious liability failed as a matter of law.

baker-v-state-318-ga-431.title: Baker v. State
baker-v-state-318-ga-431.summary: Morgan Baker was convicted of malice murder after a nightclub shooting that followed an argument with security guards, and the State introduced a 33-second clip from a rap video showing Baker holding a pistol with an extended magazine. On appeal, Baker argued that the video should have been excluded under Rule 403 because its slight probative value was overwhelmed by the risk that the jury would treat it as violent-character evidence. The Supreme Court of Georgia agreed and reversed. It held that the clip did little to prove identity or any disputed issue beyond Baker's association with the performer, while carrying substantial unfair-prejudice risk by portraying him with the kind of weapon the State wanted jurors to connect to the crime. Because the State did not show that the evidentiary error was harmless, the conviction could not stand.

baker-v-state.title: Baker v. State
baker-v-state.summary: Morgan Baker was convicted of malice murder after a nightclub shooting that followed an argument with security guards, and the State introduced a 33-second clip from a rap video showing Baker holding a pistol with an extended magazine. On appeal, Baker argued that the video should have been excluded under Rule 403 because its slight probative value was overwhelmed by the risk that the jury would treat it as violent-character evidence. The Supreme Court of Georgia agreed and reversed. It held that the clip did little to prove identity or any disputed issue beyond Baker's association with the performer, while carrying substantial unfair-prejudice risk by portraying him with the kind of weapon the State wanted jurors to connect to the crime. Because the State did not show that the evidentiary error was harmless, the conviction could not stand.

ball-rodriquez-v-progressive-premier-ins.title: Ball-Rodriquez v. Progressive Premier Insurance Company of Illinois
ball-rodriquez-v-progressive-premier-ins.summary: Francisco Ball-Rodriquez and William Stephens sought coverage under an auto policy issued by Progressive after a collision, but Progressive rescinded the policy when Stephens's bank dishonored the initial premium draft and Stephens never cured the nonpayment. The issue was whether the policy ever became enforceable despite the failed initial electronic payment and Progressive's later rescission notice. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed summary judgment for Progressive. It held that the policy expressly conditioned coverage on the financial institution honoring the initial payment and allowed Progressive to deem the policy void from inception if the payment failed. Because no valid initial premium was ever paid, there was no consideration supporting the policy and no coverage available for the later accident.

ballinger-v-watkins-315-ga-369.title: Ballinger v. Watkins
ballinger-v-watkins-315-ga-369.summary: Joseph Watkins sought habeas relief after learning that a juror conducted an unauthorized drive test during deliberations to see whether Watkins could have physically made it to the murder scene in time. The issue was whether that outside experiment created actual prejudice sufficient to upset the conviction. The Supreme Court of Georgia held that it did and affirmed the grant of habeas relief. The court reasoned that the juror introduced extra-record evidence on a sharply disputed factual point and that the timing mattered: the jury convicted the morning after the test. Because the experiment effectively supplied new proof on a contested issue without confrontation or cross-examination, the prejudice was not speculative.

bcg-operations-llc-v-town-of-homer-366-g.title: BCG Operations, LLC v. Town of Homer
bcg-operations-llc-v-town-of-homer-366-g.summary: BCG built a golf-clubhouse project in reliance on the Town of Homer's apparent ability to issue liquor-by-the-drink licenses, only to have the town clerk reject its liquor-license application after the town attorney concluded the ordinance was ultra vires. BCG obtained mandamus compelling the town to process and grant the application, then sought damages for the earlier refusal while the town pursued a cross-appeal from the mandamus ruling. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed the denial of BCG's motion for partial summary judgment on damages and dismissed the town's cross-appeal as moot. It reasoned that once the writ had already required the town to grant the license, the cross-appeal no longer presented a live controversy, and BCG had not shown entitlement to damages as a matter of law on the record before the court. The result left the mandamus relief intact but denied BCG an immediate damages win.

behl-v-state-315-ga-814.title: Behl v. State
behl-v-state-315-ga-814.summary: Edward Behl was convicted of felony murder after fatally stabbing another resident of a homeless encampment following a confrontation that began when the victim learned Behl had touched him sexually while he was passed out. On appeal, Behl argued that the jury should have been instructed on voluntary manslaughter and that his decision to represent himself while jailed left him without meaningful access to digital discovery. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed. It held that the omission of a voluntary-manslaughter charge was not plain error because the record showed a substantial cooling-off period and no serious provocation of the kind that required that lesser-offense instruction. The court also concluded that Behl failed to preserve the discovery-access complaint for appellate review, so neither issue justified a new trial.

body-v-state-367-ga-app-506.title: Body v. State
body-v-state-367-ga-app-506.summary: Jamal Body was convicted of robbery by intimidation after a street fight in which the victim said Body grabbed his phone and wallet while others also drove off in the victim's car. On appeal, Body did not attack the sufficiency of the evidence; instead, he argued that the trial court should have granted mistrials after a police officer gave testimony that allegedly violated the court's instructions and improperly put Body's character in issue. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that the trial court acted within its discretion in managing the officer's testimony and in deciding that the challenged remarks did not require the drastic remedy of a mistrial. Because the court found no reversible prejudice from the testimony, Body's conviction stood.

bonilla-v-ventura-378-ga-app-299.title: Bonilla v. Ventura
bonilla-v-ventura-378-ga-app-299.summary: After a fatal automobile collision, the decedent's parents sent Liberty Mutual a pre-suit offer under OCGA 9-11-67.1 demanding policy limits, written acceptance within thirty days, and payment within forty-five days. Liberty Mutual accepted in writing within six days and sent the check nine days after receiving the offer, but the parents later argued that this early payment did not match the offer's precise terms and filed suit anyway. The issue on interlocutory review was whether payment made before the forty-fifth day failed to create a binding settlement. The Court of Appeals held that it did not. It ruled that the offer required payment no later than forty-five days after receipt, not payment on the forty-fifth day exactly, and that early performance satisfied rather than rejected the demand. The governing rule is that settlement offers governed by OCGA 9-11-67.1 are enforced according to their precise language, but courts will not invent extra timing conditions that the offer itself does not impose. Because Liberty Mutual complied with the acceptance terms as written, the trial court should have enforced the settlement, and its contrary ruling was reversed.

brailsford-v-state-366-ga-app-331.title: Brailsford v. State
brailsford-v-state-366-ga-app-331.summary: Brandoyn Brailsford answered an undercover prostitution advertisement, agreed to pay for sex, was told the purported prostitute was actually 14 years old, replied "Ok cool," and still drove to the arranged meeting place where police arrested him. A jury convicted him of trafficking a person for sexual servitude and criminal attempt to commit aggravated child molestation. On appeal, Brailsford argued that the evidence was insufficient and that the trial court erred when it recharged the jury on criminal attempt. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that the communications and Brailsford's arrival at the park authorized the jury to find that he knowingly solicited a person he believed was a minor for commercial sex and took a substantial step toward child molestation. The court also concluded that the recharge correctly stated the law and did not mislead the jury.

bray-v-watkins-317-ga-703.title: Bray v. Watkins
bray-v-watkins-317-ga-703.summary: Latoya Bray sued a sheriff's lieutenant for allegedly failing to activate a tornado-warning system, and the lower courts treated the public duty doctrine as a threshold merits issue that could defeat the suit without first resolving sovereign-immunity questions. The Supreme Court of Georgia took the case on certiorari after the Court of Appeals split over whether the official-capacity claim needed sovereign-immunity analysis before any merits ruling. The key issue was order of operations: whether a court may bypass sovereign immunity and decide duty first when official-capacity claims are in the case. The Supreme Court said no, vacated the Court of Appeals's judgment, and remanded with direction. It explained that sovereign immunity is a jurisdictional threshold that must be addressed before a court reaches the merits, so any reliance on older language suggesting otherwise could not survive the Court's later immunity cases.

brixmor-new-chastain-corners-sc-llc-v-ja.title: Brixmor New Chastain Corners SC, LLC v. James
brixmor-new-chastain-corners-sc-llc-v-ja.summary: Arlene James tripped at night over light-colored parking bumpers set sideways beside a shopping-center parking space after the owner had replaced earlier red-and-yellow bumpers with ones left in their natural color. The property owner sought summary judgment, arguing that wheel stops are not hazards as a matter of law and that James should have known about them because she had already walked past them, and it also challenged a spoliation sanction imposed below. The Court of Appeals of Georgia rejected the owner's broad premises-liability arguments but found error in the sanction ruling. It held that whether these unusually placed, poorly contrasted bumpers created a hazard and whether James had constructive knowledge remained jury questions under the facts of this case. But because the trial court applied the wrong legal standard on spoliation, that portion of the order was vacated and remanded for reconsideration.

brown-v-state-368-ga-app-747.title: Brown v. State
brown-v-state-368-ga-app-747.summary: Rickie Lee Brown tried to cash a check drawn on Coleman Lumber's account, using the company's identifying information without authorization, and police caught him as he left the bank after the teller verified the check was bogus. Brown argued on appeal that the evidence was insufficient because the State failed to prove Coleman Lumber was a statutory "person" within Georgia's identity-fraud law. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that the trial evidence identifying Coleman Lumber Company, Inc. as a business owned and operated by Larry Coleman was enough for a rational jury to treat the company as a qualifying person under the criminal code definitions. Because the State proved Brown used the company's identifying information without consent and with fraudulent intent, the conviction stood.

brown-v-state-367-ga-app-114.title: Brown v. State
brown-v-state-367-ga-app-114.summary: Dwight Brown entered a non-negotiated guilty plea to misdemeanor reckless conduct, but before sentencing the State learned the district attorney intended to indict the same conduct as a felony and asked the trial court to dismiss the state-court case. Brown argued on appeal that once the plea was accepted, jeopardy attached and the court could not dismiss the prosecution without his consent. The Court of Appeals of Georgia did not reach the merits because it found the issue unpreserved. It held that Brown failed to make a timely and specific objection when the State requested dismissal, so the trial court was never asked to rule on the precise double-jeopardy and statutory-dismissal theory he later pressed on appeal. With no properly preserved objection, the dismissal order was affirmed.

brown-v-state.title: Brown v. State
brown-v-state.summary: Drevoisier Brown was convicted of armed robbery, aggravated assault, a gang offense, and trafficking methamphetamine after a convenience-store robbery led police to a house containing the victim?s property, incriminating clothing, drugs, cash, and a mask carrying Brown?s DNA. On appeal, Brown challenged the sufficiency of the evidence, the admission of tracking-dog testimony, and the removal of a juror who appeared inattentive and engaged with a smartwatch during trial. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed most of the case but reversed the trafficking conviction. It held that the robbery and gang evidence was ample, that the tracking-dog testimony was admissible because it merely explained how officers found the mask rather than serving as substantive proof of guilt, and that the juror?s removal was within the trial court?s discretion. But the evidence was insufficient to tie Brown to possession of the methamphetamine found in the house, so that conviction and the merged drug counts had to be vacated and the case remanded for resentencing.

brown-v-warden-2023-u-s-app-lexis-34471.title: Brown v. Warden
brown-v-warden-2023-u-s-app-lexis-34471.summary: Christopher Brown, a Georgia prisoner, sued a correctional officer under Section 1983 after she pepper-sprayed his cell when he refused to be handcuffed for a new cell assignment and later allegedly failed to secure adequate medical care for resulting vision problems. The district court entered summary judgment for the officer, and Brown appealed. The issues were whether the use of pepper spray constituted excessive force under the Eighth Amendment and whether the officer acted with deliberate indifference to Brown's medical needs. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed. It held that using pepper spray after repeated orders to cuff up and continued refusal could be viewed as a good-faith effort to restore order rather than malicious force, and that the officer was not deliberately indifferent because she escorted Brown to decontaminate once he complied and had no later role in his treatment beyond transport.

buchanan-v-hannon-366-ga-app-769.title: Buchanan v. Hannon
buchanan-v-hannon-366-ga-app-769.summary: A dispute over Gloster Buchanan's will turned on whether his home passed entirely to his daughter or was split between the daughter and a niece after his wife died before him. The trial court found an irreconcilable conflict between different sentences in the will and granted summary judgment to the niece. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed and directed judgment for the daughter. It held that the will had to be read as a whole and construed to give effect to the testator's evident intent rather than allowing one later clause to nullify the specific devise to the daughter. Because the better reading of the homeplace provision gave the daughter the residence once the wife predeceased the testator, the niece was not entitled to share in it.

burnette-v-state-368-ga-app-184.title: Burnette v. State
burnette-v-state-368-ga-app-184.summary: Deauntre Burnette was convicted of trafficking a minor for sexual servitude, pimping a person under 18, and keeping a place of prostitution after a 16-year-old victim advertised sex acts online from a hotel room registered to him and customers contacted Burnette's phone number. On appeal, Burnette challenged only the trafficking count, arguing that the State failed to prove he "provided" the victim for sexual servitude within the meaning of the statute. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that the evidence showed Burnette facilitated the victim's commercial sex work by arranging the room, using his phone as the customer contact, directing the encounters, and serving as the operational link between the ads and the buyers. That conduct was enough for a rational jury to find trafficking for sexual servitude beyond a reasonable doubt.

burroughs-v-strength-of-nature-global-ll.title: Burroughs v. Strength of Nature Global, LLC
burroughs-v-strength-of-nature-global-ll.summary: Kiara Burroughs alleged that years of using multiple brands and units of chemical hair relaxers caused her uterine fibroids and sued the manufacturers under Georgia strict-products-liability law. The manufacturers argued that all strict-liability claims were barred by the ten-year statute of repose because Burroughs first bought their products long before she filed suit. The Supreme Court of Georgia rejected that all-or-nothing theory and reversed in part. It held that OCGA ? 51-1-11(b)(2) applies on a per-unit basis, meaning the repose period runs separately from each sale of each product unit sold new to the end user. That means older units may be time-barred while claims tied to units sold within ten years can still proceed, leaving causation for later proof rather than pleading-stage dismissal.

burts-v-city-of-atlanta.title: Burts v. City of Atlanta
burts-v-city-of-atlanta.summary: Ashlee Burts sued the City of Atlanta after a collision with a City police vehicle and was met with a motion to dismiss based on the ante litem notice statute. Her follow-up notice offered to settle for the City?s liability policy limits or $250,000, whichever was greater, and the City argued that this was not a sufficiently specific damages demand. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed the dismissal. It held that the notice stated one definite settlement amount, not two competing numbers, because the restrictive clause identified a single finite figure that the City itself could determine from its own insurance information. Given Georgia?s substantial-compliance approach to municipal ante litem notices, the demand was specific enough to function as a valid offer of compromise.

bustamente-v-state.title: Bustamente v. State
bustamente-v-state.summary: Elias Bustamente was convicted of malice murder and other offenses after a drunken argument at a July 4 gathering escalated into a shooting that killed Michael Arreola, followed by Bustamente shooting himself in the head. He did not challenge his convictions. Instead, he sought a new sentencing hearing, arguing that the trial judge violated due process by warning that an apology could hurt his appeal and then imposing sentence after referring to Bustamente's lack of remorse. The Supreme Court of Georgia rejected that claim and affirmed. The issue was whether the sentencing colloquy effectively penalized Bustamente for exercising his right not to speak. The Court held that it did not: the judge explained Bustamente's right to allocute or remain silent, and the record did not show that the sentence was increased because he chose silence rather than because of the overall facts and the court's assessment of the case. The governing rule is that sentencing becomes unconstitutional only when the court actually punishes a defendant for exercising a protected right, not when the court accurately explains the strategic risks of making a statement on appeal.

cambre-assocs-llc-v-lazenby.title: Cambre & Assocs., LLC v. Lazenby
cambre-assocs-llc-v-lazenby.summary: A personal-injury lawyer filed a proposed class action against a competing firm and its personnel, alleging they illegally obtained accident-victim information and used runners to solicit clients before ethical lawyers could reach them. He framed the case as both a civil claim for interference with honest competition and a Georgia RICO claim based on unauthorized computer access to private data. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed the denial of the defendants? motion to dismiss. It held first that Georgia law does not recognize a private civil claim built out of alleged violations of criminal solicitation statutes and bar rules when those provisions do not themselves create civil remedies. It also held that the RICO theory failed because the alleged injury to competing lawyers was too indirect and speculative; the asserted predicate acts were aimed at accident victims? data, not directly at the plaintiff?s property, so proximate cause was missing.

campbell-v-universal-city-dev-partners-l.title: Campbell v. Universal City Dev. Partners, LTD
campbell-v-universal-city-dev-partners-l.summary: Dylan Campbell, who was born without one hand, was barred from riding Universal's Krakatau Aqua Coaster because the ride manufacturer required riders to have two natural grasping hands. Universal argued that Florida law and industry rules made compliance with that manufacturer restriction "necessary" under the ADA. The Eleventh Circuit disagreed with that framing and vacated summary judgment for Universal. It held that compliance with state law, standing alone, cannot automatically make a discriminatory eligibility criterion necessary when doing so would let state requirements gut the ADA. On remand, Universal would have to prove some other ADA-recognized necessity for excluding Campbell or permit him to ride.

carson-v-monsanto-co-72-f-4th-1261.title: Carson v. Monsanto Co.
carson-v-monsanto-co-72-f-4th-1261.summary: John Carson alleged that decades of Roundup use caused his cancer and sued Monsanto for failing to warn users under Georgia law. The district court dismissed the warning-based claims as expressly preempted by FIFRA because the EPA had approved Roundup labels without a cancer warning and had classified glyphosate as not likely carcinogenic. Sitting en banc, the Eleventh Circuit did not decide the ultimate preemption question but rejected the district court's shortcut. It held that whether Carson's claims are preempted must be resolved through ordinary statutory interpretation, not through a categorical rule asking whether the EPA actions had the force of law. The court remanded to the original panel to decide, under that framework, whether FIFRA actually preempts the Georgia failure-to-warn claims.

carter-v-wal-mart-stores-e-lp-2023-u-s-a.title: Carter v. Wal-Mart Stores E., LP
carter-v-wal-mart-stores-e-lp-2023-u-s-a.summary: Victoria Carter slipped in spilled soda in a Wal-Mart produce section about twelve minutes after a child created the puddle, and during the litigation Wal-Mart submitted, then withdrew, a store-employee declaration that the employee later said she had not understood. Carter appealed both the summary judgment on her premises claim and the denial of sanctions. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed on both issues. It held that under Georgia premises-liability law the short time between spill and fall was insufficient to establish Wal-Mart's constructive knowledge as a matter of law, regardless of whether employees had followed every internal inspection policy that evening. The court also agreed that sanctions were unwarranted because, although counsel's conduct with the declaration was improper, the affidavit did not affect the summary-judgment outcome and therefore caused no material prejudice to Carter.

charles-v-state-315-ga-651.title: Charles v. State
charles-v-state-315-ga-651.summary: Fred Jason Charles was convicted of malice murder and related crimes after his girlfriend was found shot to death in his bedroom and her vehicle was later discovered burned. On appeal, he challenged the sufficiency of the evidence, the trial court's handling of a potential juror irregularity, the refusal to bifurcate charges requiring proof of his felony status, and trial counsel's failure to object to the use of felon-in-possession as a felony-murder predicate. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed across the board. It held that the circumstantial and forensic evidence authorized the jury's verdict, that the juror issue was handled adequately, and that the trial court did not err in trying the charges together. The court also concluded that Charles failed to show ineffective assistance on the predicate-felony issue.

city-of-alpharetta-v-francis-366-ga-app-.title: City of Alpharetta v. Francis
city-of-alpharetta-v-francis-366-ga-app-.summary: After storm-water flooding damaged a home, the plaintiffs sued the City of Alpharetta and others, but their ante litem letter to the City listed broad damage ranges rather than a precise monetary amount. The City moved to dismiss, arguing that the notice failed to satisfy OCGA 36-33-5 (e). The Court of Appeals of Georgia largely agreed and reversed most of the trial court's refusal to dismiss. It held that the statute required a specific amount of monetary damages, not an estimate "between" two numbers or damages to be proven later at trial, so the negligence-based claims against the City could not proceed. The court remanded only the inverse-condemnation issue because the trial court had not yet decided whether that claim was even subject to the municipal ante litem statute.

city-of-brookhaven-v-multiplex-llc-369-g.title: City of Brookhaven v. Multiplex, LLC
city-of-brookhaven-v-multiplex-llc-369-g.summary: Brookhaven sued a contractor for liquidated damages after a park-construction project ran late, relying on a scope-of-work clause that tied delay damages to a deadline measured from a written notice to proceed. The city never issued that written notice, even though the project moved forward and later slipped well past the hoped-for completion date. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed summary judgment for the contractor. It held that the city could not enforce the delay clause as written because the triggering event specified in the contract never occurred. Without the required written notice to proceed, the liquidated-damages provision never became operative, so the city's contract claim failed.

city-of-milton-v-chang-2026-ga-lexis-86.title: City of Milton v. Chang
city-of-milton-v-chang-2026-ga-lexis-86.summary: Joshua Chang died after his car left Batesville Road in Milton, traveled more than sixty feet off the pavement, and struck a concrete planter located beyond the roadway. His parents won a substantial jury verdict on negligence and nuisance theories, and the Court of Appeals affirmed on the ground that Milton's immunity was waived for a road defect. The Supreme Court of Georgia took the case to decide how OCGA 36-33-1(b) and OCGA 32-4-93(a) interact and to define the scope of a city's ministerial duty to keep streets safe for travel. The Court held that the road-defect statute does not itself waive municipal immunity and that any waiver must come from another source, including the ministerial-duty waiver in OCGA 36-33-1(b) where applicable. It further held that this ministerial duty is limited to keeping lanes of ordinary travel safe and does not extend to property outside those lanes that a vehicle reaches only after leaving the road. Because the Court of Appeals treated the off-road planter as falling within the ministerial-duty waiver without analyzing the remaining immunity and nuisance questions under the correct framework, the judgment was vacated and the case was remanded.

city-of-roswell-v-hernandez-flores-365-g.title: City of Roswell v. Hernandez-Flores
city-of-roswell-v-hernandez-flores-365-g.summary: A pedestrian was catastrophically injured when a fleeing suspect swerved around stop sticks deployed by a Roswell officer and struck her on the sidewalk. She sued the City, arguing that sovereign immunity was waived because the injuries arose from the officer's "use" of a patrol vehicle. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed the denial of summary judgment to the City. It held that the record showed the injury arose from the suspect's evasive driving and the officer's deployment of stop sticks after exiting his patrol car, not from the actionable use of the city vehicle itself within the meaning of the waiver statute. Because the plaintiff failed to prove a valid waiver of sovereign immunity, the negligence action against the City could not proceed.

city-of-vidalia-v-johnson-375-ga-app-601.title: City of Vidalia v. Johnson
city-of-vidalia-v-johnson-375-ga-app-601.summary: Amy Johnson sued the City of Vidalia after tripping on an uneven sidewalk while running and claimed both negligence and nuisance. The trial court found enough evidence of constructive notice and denied summary judgment, but the Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed. It held that Johnson failed to produce evidence from which a jury could infer the City had actual or constructive notice of the specific defect, because she relied largely on photographs taken months later without contextual proof showing how long the condition had existed. The court also held that the nuisance theory failed as a matter of law because the record showed, at most, ordinary negligence and not the kind of continuous, known, and aggravated condition required to impose municipal nuisance liability.

cohen-v-progressive-mt-ins-co-375-ga-app.title: Cohen v. Progressive Mt. Ins. Co.
cohen-v-progressive-mt-ins-co-375-ga-app.summary: After a fatal motor-vehicle collision, the Cohens sent Progressive a policy-limits settlement demand two days after the uninsured-motorist carrier, Travelers, had filed an answer in the underlying tort action but before the tort defendant herself answered. Progressive argued that the demand had to comply with OCGA ? 9-11-67.1 because the statute applies before the filing of the defendant?s answer. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed the declaratory judgment entered for Progressive. Reading the statute according to its plain text, the court held that ?an answer? means any answer, not only the tort defendant?s answer. Because Travelers had already answered when the settlement letter was sent, the statutory pre-answer demand requirements no longer applied.

coleman-v-state.title: Coleman v. State
coleman-v-state.summary: Amanda Coleman appealed her malice-murder conviction for the death of two-year-old Brooklyn Aldridge, arguing that the jury should not have heard evidence of her methamphetamine use, that the defense expert was improperly restricted from offering alternative causes of death, and that trial counsel was ineffective for not objecting when the prosecutor discussed reasonable doubt in closing. The case reached the Supreme Court of Georgia after a Coffee County jury convicted Coleman and the trial court denied post-trial relief. The issues were whether the methamphetamine evidence was admissible, whether the expert-testimony ruling amounted to reversible error, and whether counsel's failure to object undermined confidence in the verdict. The Court answered all three questions against Coleman and affirmed. It held that the drug-use evidence was relevant to the State's theory of neglect and impaired caregiving, that the trial court did not plainly err in limiting speculative expert testimony about other causes of death, and that Coleman could not show Strickland prejudice from the unchallenged closing remark because the jury received the correct charge and the overall evidence supported the verdict.

cook-rose-v-waffle-house-inc.title: Cook-Rose v. Waffle House, Inc.
cook-rose-v-waffle-house-inc.summary: After plaintiffs voluntarily dismissed a premises-liability suit and then filed a renewal action, the trial court in the dismissed case awarded Waffle House more than $100,000 in attorney fees under OCGA 9-15-14. The plaintiffs sought discretionary review, but the Court of Appeals dismissed the application on the theory that the renewal action meant the original case was still pending and therefore the fee award was interlocutory absent a certificate of immediate review. The question before the Supreme Court of Georgia was whether a renewal action keeps the dismissed case alive for purposes of finality under OCGA 5-6-34 (a) (1). The Court held that it does not and reversed. It explained that a renewal suit is a new case, not a continuation of the old one, so once the fee order disposed of the dismissed case, that case was no longer pending below. Because the fee award was a final judgment in the original case, no certificate of immediate review was required and the discretionary application should have been considered on the merits.

cornejo-v-allen-367-ga-app-618.title: Cornejo v. Allen
cornejo-v-allen-367-ga-app-618.summary: Efren Cornejo sued his neighbor after the neighbor's dog first bit him and then, the next day, charged at him again, causing him to fall and injure his shoulder while trying to escape. The trial court granted summary judgment on both strict-liability and negligence theories. The Court of Appeals of Georgia split the result. It affirmed dismissal of the strict-liability claim because Georgia does not impose strict liability on dog owners in these circumstances, but it revived the negligence claim. The court held that the first day's scratch-and-bite incident, which occurred in the owner's presence, created a genuine issue of fact about whether the owner knew of the dog's vicious propensity before the second-day charging incident.

csx-corp-v-oglesby-371-ga-app-782.title: CSX Corp. v. Oglesby
csx-corp-v-oglesby-371-ga-app-782.summary: A wood-products supervisor was injured while moving a railcar that had been spotted on a private storage track, and he sued CSX claiming the railroad negligently parked the car in the wrong location. The trial court allowed part of the negligence case to survive, but CSX took an interlocutory appeal focused on duty. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed. It held that CSX had no legal duty to the plaintiff to spot the railcar in a particular place on the private track under the circumstances presented, even if better positioning might have reduced the need for later manual movement by the customer's employees. Without a recognized duty running from CSX to Oglesby on that theory, the negligence claim could not stand.

d-l-v-st-francis-health-llc-369-ga-app-3.title: D. L. v. St. Francis Health, LLC
d-l-v-st-francis-health-llc-369-ga-app-3.summary: A sedated ICU patient on a ventilator alleged that three men entered her hospital room, held her down, raped her, and later threatened her life while she remained incapacitated. The hospital won summary judgment on the ground that the assault was not foreseeable as a matter of law. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed. It held that foreseeability had to be decided by a factfinder given the patient's extreme vulnerability, the hospital's lack of meaningful controls over who entered the ICU and patient rooms, and evidence of other reported sexual-misconduct incidents at the hospital in the preceding years. Because the record did not demand a finding that the assault was unforeseeable, the premises-liability claim could not be disposed of on summary judgment.

davis-v-greensboro-estates-llc-366-ga-ap.title: Davis v. Greensboro Estates, LLC
davis-v-greensboro-estates-llc-366-ga-ap.summary: Residents of a mobile-home park were injured when an oak tree fell onto their leased home in the middle of the night, and they sued the park owner and manager for negligence, misrepresentation, punitive damages, and attorney fees. The trial court granted summary judgment across the board. The Court of Appeals of Georgia split the result. It held that evidence about the condition of the tree and the defendants' knowledge of complaints created a jury issue on simple and gross negligence, so those claims should not have been dismissed. But it agreed that the plaintiffs lacked evidence of an actionable false statement supporting negligent misrepresentation. Because the negligence claims survived, the derivative punitive-damages and litigation-expense claims also had to be revived.

delaney-v-state-366-ga-app-707.title: Delaney v. State
delaney-v-state-366-ga-app-707.summary: Yvonne Delaney, who had been found not guilty by reason of insanity years earlier, appealed an order continuing her unsupervised conditional release instead of fully discharging her from commitment. She argued that she had satisfied the terms of her release plan, rebutted any presumption that she still required involuntary treatment, and presented evidence supporting full release. The Court of Appeals of Georgia vacated and remanded with direction. It held that the record was too incomplete and the trial court's analysis too unclear to support continued conditional release on the terms entered below, especially given prior expert testimony that Delaney no longer met inpatient or outpatient commitment criteria. The case was sent back for the trial court to properly address the statutory standards on an adequate record.

drucker-v-morgan-371-ga-app-334.title: Drucker v. Morgan
drucker-v-morgan-371-ga-app-334.summary: An Airbnb guest in Savannah was seriously injured when an overhanging, long-dead tree limb fell onto her after her husband lightly tugged on a hanging vine near the rental's advertised parking area. The trial court granted summary judgment to the property owner, his LLC, and the property-management company. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed in part and reversed in part. It held that the evidence, including an arborist's testimony about obvious decay and prior storm damage, created a jury issue as to whether at least some defendants had superior knowledge of a dangerous condition over the designated parking area. But the court also recognized limits on liability depending on ownership and control of the exact area where the injury occurred, so not every defendant necessarily remained in the case on the same footing.

eubanks-v-state-317-ga-563.title: Eubanks v. State
eubanks-v-state-317-ga-563.summary: Jessica Eubanks kept a large amount of heroin in a home shared with a severely developmentally disabled woman, conducted a heroin sale there, spilled the drug in accessible areas, and then left the vulnerable victim home alone; the next day the victim was dead from heroin toxicity. Eubanks challenged her felony-murder conviction on the ground that possession with intent to distribute heroin was not inherently dangerous under these facts and did not proximately cause the death. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed. It held that, on these unusual facts, keeping and distributing the drug in a home where a highly vulnerable resident could foreseeably be exposed made the predicate felony dangerous to human life and supported proximate cause. The court also rejected her jury-charge, demurrer, hearsay, and evidentiary arguments.

fleureme-v-city-of-atlanta-2026-ga-app-l.title: Fleureme v. City of Atlanta
fleureme-v-city-of-atlanta-2026-ga-app-l.summary: This follow-on appeal came back to the Court of Appeals after the Supreme Court of Georgia reversed its earlier decision in Fleureme?s suit against the City of Atlanta. The dispute centered on whether an ante litem notice delivered to the ?Office of the Mayor? satisfied OCGA ? 36-33-5(f). The issue on remand was narrow because the Supreme Court had already answered the controlling notice question. The Court of Appeals vacated its prior judgment and made the Supreme Court?s ruling the judgment of the court. It therefore reversed the trial court?s dismissal to the extent that dismissal rested on the view that notice to the mayor?s office was legally insufficient.

fleureme-v-city-of-atlanta.title: Fleureme v. City of Atlanta
fleureme-v-city-of-atlanta.summary: Roodson Fleureme alleged that he was injured by a City of Atlanta vehicle and, before filing suit, sent multiple timely ante litem notices by Federal Express, including one addressed to the Office of the Mayor at the correct City Hall address. The lower courts dismissed the case on the theory that OCGA 36-33-5 (f) required service on the mayor personally and by name. The question before the Supreme Court of Georgia was whether a notice sent by statutory overnight delivery to the mayor's office at the correct address can satisfy the statute's service requirement. The Court held that it can. The governing rule is that the ante litem service provision must be read according to its text and context, and the statute does not impose a hyper-technical naming requirement that defeats otherwise proper delivery to the correct mayoral office. Because the Court of Appeals adopted an unduly rigid reading of the service statute, its judgment was reversed and the case was remanded.

floyd-v-state-318-ga-312.title: Floyd v. State
floyd-v-state-318-ga-312.summary: Darnell Floyd was acquitted of malice murder and several assault-based counts but convicted of felony murder predicated on possession of a firearm by a convicted felon after a shooting in which he claimed self-defense. On appeal, he argued that trial counsel mishandled the legal interplay between self-defense and the felon-in-possession charge in multiple critical ways. The Supreme Court of Georgia agreed in part and reversed the challenged convictions. It held that counsel rendered constitutionally ineffective assistance in dealing with the justification issues tied to the firearm-possession count, and that those failures undermined confidence in the result on the felony-murder theory that depended on that predicate offense. Because the evidence was otherwise sufficient, however, the State may retry Floyd.

freeman-v-state-367-ga-app-57.title: Freeman v. State
freeman-v-state-367-ga-app-57.summary: Christopher Freeman was convicted after a brutal home invasion in which the victim was attacked, raped, dragged through her house, threatened, and robbed while alone late at night. On appeal, Freeman challenged the sufficiency of the evidence on aggravated assault, the denial of a motion in limine, and the effectiveness of trial counsel. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that the victim's account and the surrounding physical and forensic evidence were enough to support the assault finding, that the trial court did not reversibly err in its evidentiary ruling, and that Freeman failed to establish ineffective assistance under the governing standard. None of the asserted errors warranted a new trial.

frey-v-jesperson-366-ga-app-488.title: Frey v. Jesperson
frey-v-jesperson-366-ga-app-488.summary: After William Frey died in a motorcycle crash, his widow argued that Liberty Mutual owed the broadest form of uninsured-motorist coverage because its UM selection materials were confusing, coercive, and not timely accepted. Liberty Mutual contended that William knowingly chose reduced-by coverage, which cut the available benefits after Progressive paid its share. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed summary judgment for Liberty Mutual. It held that the cover letter and selection form did not unlawfully coerce William into a lower option, that he had a meaningful chance to select different coverage, and that his late return of the form still created a valid agreement on the chosen terms. Because the record showed a knowing and voluntary election of reduced-by coverage, Liberty Mutual owed only the lower amount it had already paid.

fulton-county-bd-of-comm-rs-v-fulton-cou.title: Fulton County Bd. of Comm'Rs v. Fulton County Republican Party Two Cases
fulton-county-bd-of-comm-rs-v-fulton-cou.summary: The Fulton County Republican Party obtained a writ of mandamus requiring the Fulton County Board of Commissioners to seat two of its nominees on the county Board of Registration and Elections, and later obtained a contempt order when the Commissioners refused to do so. The appeals asked whether a local act stating that certain board members shall be appointed from party nominations stripped the Commissioners of all discretion and allowed the courts to compel the appointments. The Court of Appeals answered no. It held that the Act limited the pool of possible appointees to party nominees, but it did not convert the Commissioners' appointment power into a purely ministerial duty subject to judicial direction absent a gross abuse of discretion. The controlling rule is that mandamus does not lie to control a discretionary public appointment decision unless the governing body has clearly abused that discretion, and longstanding Georgia law treats appointment power as a core governmental prerogative. Because the trial court improperly interfered with that discretionary authority, the writ of mandamus was reversed, and the contempt order fell with it.

ga-cvs-pharm-llc-v-carmichael-316-ga-718.title: Ga. CVS Pharm., LLC v. Carmichael
ga-cvs-pharm-llc-v-carmichael-316-ga-718.summary: These consolidated cases asked the Supreme Court of Georgia to clarify the principles governing liability for injuries caused by third-party criminal acts on business premises. The appeals involved different facts and defendants, including owners, occupiers, and security contractors, but all turned on the same core question: when does Georgia premises law impose a duty to protect invitees from crime committed by others. The Supreme Court used the cases to restate and organize the governing framework. It emphasized that foreseeability remains central, that duty analysis must track the specific role of each defendant, and that negligent-security claims cannot skip over the ordinary elements of premises liability. Applying those principles, the Court affirmed one judgment, reversed in part and vacated in part the others, and remanded for proceedings under the clarified standards.

ga-div-sons-of-federate-veterans-v-downs.title: Ga. Div., Sons of Confederate Veterans v. Downs
ga-div-sons-of-federate-veterans-v-downs.summary: Organizations and individuals aligned with the Sons of Confederate Veterans sued after Decatur and DeKalb County removed a Confederate monument from the city square under an earlier court order. After an earlier standing ruling was disrupted by the Supreme Court's later monument cases, the Court of Appeals revisited which plaintiffs could proceed. It held that some plaintiffs still lacked standing, but that two individual plaintiffs had alleged enough to survive dismissal on that ground. The court then vacated in part and remanded because the trial court had never resolved the threshold question whether sovereign immunity barred the remaining claims. The result narrowed the plaintiff group and sent the case back for the jurisdictional immunity analysis that had to be addressed before the merits.

garren-v-bryant.title: Garren v. Bryant
garren-v-bryant.summary: After inmate Larry Burden died following repeated complaints of chest pain and a collapse in the prison yard, his mother sued several correctional officers for wrongful death, alleging they failed to carry out ministerial emergency-response duties imposed by prison policies and training. The officers claimed official immunity. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed in part and reversed in part. It held that some of the alleged emergency-response obligations, including duties tied to checking and having an AED available, were sufficiently specific to support ministerial-duty claims against most of the officers, so dismissal on immunity grounds was not warranted across the board. But the court concluded that one newly hired cadet, Moore, could not be held on the same theory because there was no evidence he knew the policies or had the training that would make those duties ministerial as to him.

gates-v-state-317-ga-889.title: Gates v. State
gates-v-state-317-ga-889.summary: After a serious crash, investigators obtained Joseph Gates's hospital records, including blood-test results, through an ex parte court order rather than a warrant and then used those records to pursue DUI charges. Gates moved to suppress the records, arguing that the State violated his privacy rights under the Georgia Constitution. The Supreme Court of Georgia agreed and reversed the denial of suppression. Relying on its privacy precedent, the Court held that the State could not sidestep warrant requirements by secretly obtaining broad medical records through an ex parte order. Because the records were acquired in violation of Gates's state constitutional right to privacy, the evidence had to be suppressed.

girls-galore-inc-v-city-of-atlanta-2026-.title: Girls Galore, Inc. v. City of Atlanta
girls-galore-inc-v-city-of-atlanta-2026-.summary: Girls Galore, which operated the Allure Gentlemen's Club, sought review after the Atlanta mayor revoked its alcohol license following a show-cause hearing tied to an after-hours shooting investigation, employee-permit violations, and alleged failures to supervise staff. The case reached the Court of Appeals after the superior court affirmed the mayor's decision under SSCAPA. The central questions were whether the City gave constitutionally adequate notice of the review-board hearing, whether the superior court used the proper review standard, and whether the revocation could lawfully be treated as a second after-hours offense based on a prior violation from 2020. The Court of Appeals held that the City's delivery efforts satisfied due process, but it also held that the record supported only the current violations and not the prior-violation finding used to justify the harsher penalty. The governing rule is that an administrative sanction must rest on record-supported violations, especially when a progressive penalty scheme turns on repeat-offender status. Because the evidence did not support the prior violation that drove the revocation decision, the court vacated the superior court's affirmance and remanded for further proceedings.

greathouse-v-state.title: Greathouse v. State
greathouse-v-state.summary: Jody Greathouse repeatedly signed waiver-and-consent revocation orders while on First Offender probation for drug offenses, and after later violations the trial court revoked the balance of his probation and ordered incarceration. Greathouse moved to vacate and modify the arrest and revocation orders, arguing that the confinement ruling exceeded what the earlier waivers and statutory scheme allowed. The Court of Appeals of Georgia vacated and remanded. It held that the record and the sequence of consent revocation orders required closer scrutiny of the trial court's authority to impose full confinement under the cited probation statutes. Because the existing orders did not support the disposition with enough clarity, the case had to go back for further proceedings consistent with the proper revocation framework.

guy-v-housing-authority-of-augusta-372-g.title: Guy v. Housing Authority of Augusta
guy-v-housing-authority-of-augusta-372-g.summary: A public-housing tenant sued the Housing Authority of Augusta after unknown assailants tried to rob her on her porch and shot her in the leg. The Authority moved for summary judgment, arguing that it was protected by sovereign immunity. The Court of Appeals of Georgia agreed and affirmed. It held that in operating and managing low-income public housing, the Authority was acting as an instrumentality of the City of Augusta and performing an essential governmental function rather than a purely private one. Because no applicable waiver of sovereign immunity was shown, the premises-liability and negligent-security claims could not proceed.

hamon-v-nell-315-ga-760.title: Hamon v. Connell
hamon-v-nell-315-ga-760.summary: A daughter filed a wrongful-death medical-malpractice action for her father after alleging that his estranged surviving spouse refused to bring the claim herself. The defendants argued that the Wrongful Death Act gave only the spouse standing and sought judgment on the pleadings. The Supreme Court of Georgia reversed the Court of Appeals and held that the case could go forward. It concluded that under the specific alleged circumstances, equitable principles could permit the child to pursue the wrongful-death claim when the surviving spouse held the statutory right but refused to act, leaving no adequate remedy otherwise. Because the complaint alleged facts fitting that equitable exception, the trial court properly denied judgment on the pleadings.

hare-krishna-roswell-hotel-llc-v-corsino.title: Hare Krishna Roswell Hotel, LLC v. Corsino
hare-krishna-roswell-hotel-llc-v-corsino.summary: After an imposter used Sharon Corsino's driver's license to book a hotel room and make fraudulent purchases, police misidentified Corsino from hotel records and video and she was later arrested before the charges were dropped. Corsino sued the hotel, claiming it negligently failed to verify that the guest's identification matched the debit card used at check-in. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed the denial of summary judgment to the hotel. It held that the hotel had no legal duty to Corsino on the negligence theory she advanced, even if its internal or franchise check-in practices were imperfect. Without a duty running from the hotel to Corsino in these circumstances, her claims for the downstream harms of arrest and lost employment failed as a matter of law.

harper-v-state.title: Harper v. State
harper-v-state.summary: Genesis Harper was convicted of armed robbery and, as a recidivist, received life without parole because Georgia?s recidivist statute required the maximum punishment for the offense of conviction. Harper argued that the armed-robbery sentencing statute is ambiguous because it allows either life imprisonment or a term of years, and that the rule of lenity therefore required the lesser maximum sentence of 20 years. The Court of Appeals of Georgia rejected that argument and affirmed. It held that binding precedent already treats life imprisonment as the maximum sentence authorized by OCGA ? 16-8-41(b), with a term of years functioning as a lesser alternative rather than a competing maximum. Because the statute was not ambiguous in the relevant sense, lenity never came into play.

harris-v-state-316-ga-141.title: Harris v. State
harris-v-state-316-ga-141.summary: Evins Harris was convicted of felony murder and related crimes after shooting his girlfriend's brother inside their shared home, and he argued that he should have been immune from prosecution because he acted in self-defense. He also challenged the admission of an in-life photograph of the victim at trial. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed. It held that the trial court did not err in denying pretrial immunity because the evidence, including the physical scene and Harris's changing accounts, did not compel a finding that the shooting was justified. The court also concluded that admitting the in-life photograph was not an abuse of discretion and did not warrant reversal.

harris-v-state.title: Harris v. State
harris-v-state.summary: Emmanuel Harris was convicted of murdering his girlfriend after a domestic fight in which he claimed he acted in self-defense when she came at him with a knife. The State introduced extensive evidence that Harris had badly injured a different girlfriend years earlier, framing that incident as proof of a violent motive to control intimate partners and to rebut accident or mistake. The issue on appeal was whether that prior-abuse evidence was properly admitted under Rule 404 (b) and, if not, whether the error was harmless. The Supreme Court of Georgia held that the trial court abused its discretion and reversed. It concluded that the State's theory of a generic motive to control partners with violence was too close to forbidden propensity reasoning, that the alternative accident-or-mistake rationale carried little probative value in this record, and that the highly prejudicial proof of the earlier assault could have influenced the jury's rejection of self-defense. Because the error was not harmless, Harris is entitled to a new trial.

hartwell-r-r-co-v-hartwell-first-united-.title: Hartwell R.R. Co. v. Hartwell First United Methodist Church
hartwell-r-r-co-v-hartwell-first-united-.summary: A church and two railroad companies fought over a narrow strip of land occupied by an old spur track beside the church campus, with the central question being whether the railroad still held rights to that land. After a bench hearing, the superior court entered declaratory relief for the church and permanently enjoined the railroad companies. The Court of Appeals of Georgia largely agreed on ownership but not on the remedy. It affirmed the declaratory judgment because there was evidence supporting the trial court's conclusion about the scope of the historical railroad right-of-way and the church's superior claim to the disputed strip. But it vacated the permanent injunction, holding that the injunctive relief required a more supportable legal foundation than the record and order provided.

hightower-v-state-315-ga-399.title: Hightower v. State
hightower-v-state-315-ga-399.summary: Michelle Hightower's murder trial was underway in March 2020 when the chief judge declared a COVID-19 judicial emergency in Fulton County and the trial court, over defense objection, declared a mistrial. Hightower later filed a plea in bar arguing that retrying her would violate double jeopardy because no manifest necessity justified ending the first trial. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed the denial of that plea. It held that, in the extraordinary circumstances created by the rapidly developing COVID-19 emergency, the trial court acted within its discretion in concluding that a mistrial was necessary to protect the health of jurors, parties, witnesses, and court personnel. Because manifest necessity supported the mistrial, renewed prosecution was not barred.

hilton-v-state.title: Hilton v. State
hilton-v-state.summary: Hilton was convicted of murder after shooting Allen and argued on appeal that the jury should have been instructed on voluntary manslaughter because he had told investigators that Allen had violated and threatened him. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed. It was skeptical that Hilton?s vague post-shooting statements amounted even to slight evidence of the kind of sudden, violent passion required for voluntary manslaughter, but it did not need to resolve that point definitively. Instead, the Court held that any failure to give the instruction was harmless because the State?s evidence strongly supported malice murder and the evidence of serious provocation was weak and nonspecific. In that posture, it was highly probable that the verdict would have been the same even with the lesser-offense charge.

hoffman-v-se-ob-gyn-ctr-llc-378-ga-app-3.title: Hoffman v. Se. OB/GYN Ctr., LLC
hoffman-v-se-ob-gyn-ctr-llc-378-ga-app-3.summary: Jaime Hoffman sought a new trial after a defense verdict in her medical-malpractice suit arising from treatment of a post-operative ileus following a cesarean section. The central issue was whether the trial court should have removed for cause a prospective juror who worked as an attorney for the defendants? insurer, MagMutual, and admitted that the relationship created a substantial potential for bias. The Court of Appeals of Georgia held that the juror was disqualified and that the trial court erred by keeping him on the panel and forcing Hoffman to spend a peremptory strike to remove him. In civil cases, the parties are entitled to a full panel of 24 competent and impartial jurors, and the loss of that statutory right is presumptively harmful when the party exhausts her strikes. Because that error alone required reversal, the court ordered a new trial and did not reach Hoffman?s remaining complaint about the judge?s conduct.

holloway-v-state.title: Holloway v. State
holloway-v-state.summary: Malcolm Holloway was convicted of felony murder and related crimes after he helped organize an armed ambush of a family that owned a gas station and check-cashing business, supplied weapons, acted as lookout, and one of his co-conspirators was fatally shot during the attempted robbery. On appeal, Holloway argued that the evidence was insufficient because he was not at the scene when the shots were fired, that the jury instructions on conspiracy and proximate cause were incomplete, and that the trial court mishandled impeachment and other-acts evidence. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed. It held that the evidence authorized the jury to find Holloway was a knowing participant in the robbery conspiracy and that the fatal shooting was a foreseeable consequence of the armed plan he helped set in motion. The court also concluded that the challenged instructions did not amount to plain error and that the remaining evidentiary complaints did not warrant reversal.

holt-v-rickman-368-ga-app-55.title: Holt v. Rickman
holt-v-rickman-368-ga-app-55.summary: Brenazia Holt sought emotional-distress damages after waking up to find an apartment-complex maintenance worker in her bed, but she suffered no physical injury and sought no medical treatment after the incident. She sued the apartment owners and manager for premises liability and negligent hiring, retention, and supervision. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed summary judgment for the defendants. It held that Georgia's impact rule barred Holt's negligence-based emotional-distress claims because she could not show a qualifying physical impact that caused a physical injury and then mental suffering. Without that required physical-injury component, the negligence claims could not proceed even though the alleged conduct was deeply disturbing.

honeyfund-com-inc-v-governor-94-f-4th-12.title: Honeyfund.com Inc. v. Governor
honeyfund-com-inc-v-governor-94-f-4th-12.summary: Florida defended a law restricting mandatory workplace trainings that endorse certain views about race, sex, and national origin while allowing required trainings that reject those same views or present them "objectively." Employers and trainers challenged the law as viewpoint discrimination. The Eleventh Circuit agreed and held the statute unconstitutional. It rejected Florida's attempt to reframe the law as regulating conduct rather than speech, explaining that the Act turned directly on the viewpoint expressed in employer-mandated speech. Because the statute singled out disfavored ideas for suppression, ordinary First Amendment scrutiny applied and the law could not stand.

hostetler-v-state.title: Hostetler v. State
hostetler-v-state.summary: Rachel Hostetler completed her misdemeanor DUI sentence while her habeas petition alleging ineffective assistance was still pending, and the habeas court dismissed the case as moot once her probation ended. The issue on appeal was whether the potential for a future enhanced DUI sentence counted as an adverse collateral consequence keeping her liberty restrained under Georgia's habeas statute. The Supreme Court of Georgia said yes and vacated the dismissal. It held that the prospect of recidivist enhancement for a later DUI conviction can qualify as a continuing collateral consequence sufficient to prevent mootness, so long as it is established in the record. The case was remanded for further habeas proceedings instead of being treated as extinguished by sentence completion.

howard-v-ctw-enterprises-inc-367-ga-app-.title: Howard v. CTW Enterprises, Inc.
howard-v-ctw-enterprises-inc-367-ga-app-.summary: An elderly, longtime customer at an auto shop fell into a service pit after an employee told him to back up while crouched in front of his headlights, but the customer already knew the pit was behind him and did not look before stepping back. He sued the shop and its manager for premises liability. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed summary judgment for the defendants. It held that the pit was a static condition of which Howard had equal knowledge, if not superior familiarity, because he had visited the facility for years and understood the layout. Since Georgia law bars recovery where an invitee fails to exercise ordinary care around an open and obvious static hazard he already knows about, the claim failed.

hughes-v-ace-american-insurance-company-.title: Hughes v. Ace American Insurance Company
hughes-v-ace-american-insurance-company-.summary: Kenneth Hughes tried to bring a direct action against Ace American after a passenger van driven by an employee of a care-services provider collided with his truck, arguing that the insured entities qualified as motor carriers under Georgia law. The trial court rejected that theory and granted summary judgment to the insurer. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that the entities operating the seven-passenger van were not motor carriers within the strict statutory meaning required for Georgia's direct-action provision. Because the Motor Carrier Act must be strictly construed and the record did not bring the defendants within the statute, Ace could not be sued directly under OCGA 40-1-112.

hughes-v-state-366-ga-app-335.title: Hughes v. State
hughes-v-state-366-ga-app-335.summary: Bonnie Hughes moved to suppress evidence seized during execution of a no-knock warrant issued after controlled buys of methamphetamine from her residence. The affidavit cited surveillance cameras, daily movement of narcotics, prior knock-and-talk efforts, and general concerns that drugs can be hidden or destroyed. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed the denial of suppression. It held that the no-knock authorization rested on boilerplate and generalized assertions rather than the sort of particularized facts needed to justify dispensing with the knock-and-announce rule. Because the warrant application did not adequately show that knocking would significantly increase danger or lead to immediate destruction of evidence, the no-knock provision was invalid and the trial court should not have denied the suppression motion.

in-the-interest-of-m-b.title: In the Interest of M. B.
in-the-interest-of-m-b.summary: A mother in a private dependency case moved to recuse the juvenile-court judge after an adjudication hearing, alleging bias, ex parte conduct, and courtroom actions that she said compromised the fairness of the proceedings. After the judge denied the motion himself, the mother appealed to the Court of Appeals of Georgia. The issue was whether the judge was required to treat the affidavit's factual allegations as true for threshold purposes and refer the recusal motion to another judge instead of disputing the allegations personally. The Court of Appeals held that he was. It reversed and remanded with direction, ruling that the judge violated the governing recusal procedure by refuting the affidavit on the merits rather than passing the motion to another judge once the filings alleged facts that, if true, could support disqualification. Because the required process was not followed, the denial order could not stand and the proceedings after the motion was filed were voided for further action before a new judge.

in-the-matter-of-calhoun-317-ga-726.title: In the Matter of Calhoun
in-the-matter-of-calhoun-317-ga-726.summary: The State Bar sought to suspend lawyer W. McCall Calhoun, Jr. after a federal bench trial found him guilty of a felony obstruction count and several misdemeanor offenses arising from his participation in the January 6, 2021 breach of the United States Capitol. The issue was whether those guilty judgments, though still on direct appeal and not yet final, justified immediate attorney discipline and whether Calhoun's claimed political motives or due-process objections blocked interim suspension. The Supreme Court of Georgia suspended him until the Eleventh Circuit finishes his direct appeal and until further order of the Court. It held that the guilty verdicts qualified as convictions under the Bar Rules, that felonies are crimes of moral turpitude for disciplinary purposes, and that pending appellate review does not prevent protective suspension. The court's reasoning stressed the conclusive effect of the federal judgments, Calhoun's own testimony acknowledging he knowingly entered the Capitol for the cause, and prior Georgia precedent allowing interim suspension to protect public confidence in the legal profession.

in-the-matter-of-crawford-317-ga-297.title: In the Matter of Crawford
in-the-matter-of-crawford-317-ga-297.summary: Former superior-court judge Robert Mallory "Mack" Crawford faced consolidated bar proceedings after taking money from the Pike County court registry and mishandling client funds in the same course of conduct that had already prompted prior judicial-discipline litigation and a misdemeanor theft plea. The issues were whether the proven rules violations warranted disbarment, as the special master recommended, and whether the Court also needed to reach a separate charge tied to Crawford's first-offender Alford plea. The Supreme Court of Georgia imposed a three-year suspension and declined to decide the separate misdemeanor-based charge. It held that Crawford clearly violated multiple professional-conduct rules governing trust money and dishonest conduct, but that a lengthy suspension rather than disbarment was the appropriate sanction because the misconduct stemmed from a single course of conduct. The reasoning balanced serious aggravators, including vulnerable victims, unlawful conduct, and Crawford's refusal to acknowledge wrongdoing, against Georgia's disciplinary practice of reserving disbarment for still more extreme records and treating a three-year suspension as the heaviest ordinary suspension available.

inquiry-cerning-judge-coomer-315-ga-841.title: Inquiry Concerning Judge Coomer
inquiry-cerning-judge-coomer-315-ga-841.summary: The Judicial Qualifications Commission pursued 36 counts against Judge Christian Coomer based on alleged misconduct involving client dealings, campaign spending, fictitious transfers, and financial disclosures, and its Hearing Panel recommended removing him from the Court of Appeals. The case reached the Supreme Court of Georgia on whether the panel applied the right law when it treated pre-judicial conduct as sanctionable under the Code of Judicial Conduct and treated negligence or ignorance as enough for discipline over non-judicial acts. The Court did not resolve the merits and instead remanded with instructions. It held that the Code of Judicial Conduct applies only to conduct by judges and judicial candidates while they hold that status, and that conduct outside a judicial capacity can support discipline only if done in bad faith. The reasoning was blunt: the JQC and Hearing Panel used a legal framework inconsistent with the text of the Code and with Georgia precedent, so the removal recommendation could not stand on the existing findings.

int-l-bhd-of-police-officers-local-623-i.title: Int’l Bhd. of Police Officers Local 623, Inc. v. Brosnan
int-l-bhd-of-police-officers-local-623-i.summary: Officer Devin Brosnan sued his police union after it failed to provide the legal representation it had promised in recruiting materials and member-benefit documents when the investigation into the Rayshard Brooks shooting began. The trial court granted partial summary judgment to Brosnan on liability and left damages for a jury, and the union appealed. The issue was whether the union's written and oral promises created an enforceable obligation to provide job-related legal defense and whether the union breached that obligation when Brosnan requested counsel after a critical incident. The Court of Appeals held that they did. It concluded that the union's membership materials and related assurances were sufficiently definite to create an enforceable contract and that Brosnan's payment of dues supplied consideration for that promise. The governing rule is that organizational benefit promises can become binding contractual obligations when the terms are definite enough and the member performs in reliance by paying dues or otherwise furnishing consideration. Applying that rule, the court affirmed the liability ruling and left the amount of recoverable damages for later resolution.

jackson-v-state-318-ga-393.title: Jackson v. State
jackson-v-state-318-ga-393.summary: After a birthday-party confrontation turned into a fatal shooting, brothers Dedric Jackson and Dimitrius Castle were jointly tried and convicted of felony murder and related offenses for the death of Norman Ray Moore, Jr. On appeal, Jackson argued that trial counsel mishandled justification-related charges, bad-character evidence, witness testimony, and jury instructions, while Castle challenged the sufficiency of the evidence on a battery count, raised his own ineffective-assistance claims, and argued that aggravated assault should have merged into felony murder. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed both judgments. It held that neither defendant showed deficient performance and prejudice under Strickland, that the evidence supported Castle's battery conviction, and that merger was not required because the trial court was authorized to treat the shooting as involving separate aggravated assaults divided by a deliberate interval. The court's reasoning rested on the trial record showing tactical explanations for counsel's decisions, no sua sponte duty to give unsupported justification instructions, and witness testimony that allowed the sentencing court to find distinct assaultive acts rather than one continuous offense.

jenkins-v-state.title: Jenkins v. State
jenkins-v-state.summary: Larry Jenkins was retried for a murder he committed as a teenager after earlier post-conviction proceedings vacated his death sentence and granted a new trial, and at the retrial the State introduced custodial statements and derivative evidence after a court ruled that Jenkins had not validly invoked his Miranda right to counsel during booking. The issue was whether Jenkins's statement that he would not talk without a lawyer was an impermissible anticipatory invocation because formal interrogation was not underway at that exact moment. The Supreme Court of Georgia reversed. It held that Jenkins validly invoked his Miranda rights because a reasonable person in his position, already in custody, already warned, already questioned in transit, and then being booked, would have understood interrogation to be at least imminent. The court reasoned that once police failed to honor that unequivocal request for counsel, the resulting statements were inadmissible, and the State did not carry its burden to show harmless error.
