Because the remaining evidence was constitutionally sufficient, the State may retry him.

jennis-v-state-375-ga-app-743.title: Jennis v. State
jennis-v-state-375-ga-app-743.summary: Nidhi Jennis sought discharge and acquittal on misdemeanor traffic charges after filing a statutory speedy-trial demand that appeared as a separately titled document within a larger packet of motions. The trial court ruled that the demand was ineffective because it was buried in the packet and was not filed as a sufficiently separate document. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed. It held that the demand satisfied the plain requirements of OCGA ? 17-7-170 because it had its own title, case information, statutory citation, and certificate of service, and it was not part of another single pleading just because it was mailed alongside other motions. The case was remanded for the trial court to address the remaining statutory prerequisites under subsection (b).

jones-v-ga-farm-bureau-mut-ins-co-367-ga.title: Jones v. Ga. Farm Bureau Mut. Ins. Co.
jones-v-ga-farm-bureau-mut-ins-co-367-ga.summary: After Ernie Jones was killed in a car accident, his children disputed Georgia Farm Bureau's position that his policy carried only $25,000 in uninsured-motorist coverage rather than UM limits matching the policy's $1,000,000 liability coverage. The case turned on whether Jones had made the written election required by Georgia's UM statute to choose lower limits, and whether missing underwriting paperwork or the agent's credibility created a fact issue. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed partial summary judgment for the insurer. It held that Jones validly chose reduced UM coverage because he signed a form expressly electing UM limits lower than liability coverage, and the declaration page and subsequent policy notices fixed that selected amount at $25,000. The court reasoned that the documents themselves resolved the dispute without needing credibility calls, and that the insureds' spoliation theory failed because the discarded quote sheet was thrown away before the accident and before any litigation was pending or reasonably anticipated.

lee-v-state.title: Lee v. State
lee-v-state.summary: Kevin James Lee was convicted of murdering his wife in 1991 after she tried to flee an abusive relationship; her body was buried near the family home and not discovered until teenagers unearthed skeletal remains almost twenty years later. On appeal, Lee argued that the circumstantial evidence still left a reasonable alternative suspect, that the trial court mishandled hearsay and juror issues, and that trial counsel was ineffective for not pursuing a constitutional speedy-trial plea in bar and for mishandling a misdemeanor plea offer. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed across the board. It held that the jury was entitled to reject Lee?s theory that another man committed the murder, that the challenged relationship evidence was either properly admitted or not shown to be harmful, and that counsel was not deficient because the record undercut both the speedy-trial theory and Lee?s later claim that he misunderstood the plea decision. The court?s reasoning turned on the strength of the surrounding circumstantial evidence, the trustworthiness of the admitted statements, and the trial record showing Lee knowingly chose trial over a walk-away deal.

lewis-v-state-367-ga-app-638.title: Lewis v. State
lewis-v-state-367-ga-app-638.summary: Janice Lewis was convicted of DUI less safe after a bench trial and challenged the denial of her motion to suppress field-sobriety evidence gathered after she was stopped at a late-night roadblock. The issue was whether the checkpoint was invalid because the written approval form listed a nonexistent intersection, which Lewis argued showed that field officers rather than supervisory personnel chose the location. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that the trial court was entitled to treat the mistaken intersection on the form as a clerical error and to find, based on circumstantial evidence, that a supervisor had in fact authorized the roadblock at the actual site. The reasoning centered on the undisputed fact that the listed roads did not intersect, the officers' untroubled arrival at the actual checkpoint location, and Georgia precedent allowing minor location discrepancies so long as the decision to implement the roadblock came from supervisory personnel rather than officers in the field.

locklear-v-state-317-ga-115.title: Locklear v. State
locklear-v-state-317-ga-115.summary: Tony James Locklear was convicted of murdering 82-year-old William Long, concealing Long's death, and related offenses after police found him asleep in the victim's truck and later recovered the body based on Locklear's custodial statements. On appeal, he attacked the sufficiency of the evidence, the denial of suppression motions aimed at his statements and physical evidence, the verdict form, and the trial court's refusal to grant a mistrial after improper remarks in closing. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed across the board. It held that the evidence easily supported the convictions and that none of the challenged rulings amounted to reversible error. The court's reasoning emphasized the powerful circumstantial and direct evidence tying Locklear to the killing, the lack of a legal basis to exclude the challenged statements or seized evidence, and the limited prejudice from the prosecutor's brief improper comments in light of sustained objections and standard jury instructions that arguments are not evidence.

lodge-v-united-states-ag-92-f-4th-1298.title: Lodge v. United States AG
lodge-v-united-states-ag-92-f-4th-1298.summary: Robert Franklyn Lodge, a Jamaican-born lawful permanent resident whose father later naturalized in the United States, sought to block his removal after aggravated-felony convictions by arguing that a repealed derivative-citizenship statute unconstitutionally favored certain mothers over unmarried fathers and discriminated on racial lines. The issue was whether the Eleventh Circuit could cure the statute's alleged constitutional defects in a way that would retroactively make Lodge a citizen through his father. The court denied the petition for review. It held that even under a version of the old statute stripped of the challenged defect, Lodge still could not establish derivative citizenship, so the court could not grant the remedy he sought. The reasoning turned on redressability: because the complained-of sex classification did not actually cause Lodge's inability to derive citizenship on these facts, the court did not need to reach the broader constitutional merits and instead left the removal order in place.

love-v-mcknight-321-ga-196.title: Love v. McKnight
love-v-mcknight-321-ga-196.summary: This certiorari case grew out of a rear-end collision on Interstate 20 in which Anthony Love struck John McKnight's truck and McKnight sought not only damages but litigation expenses under OCGA 13-6-11. The trial court and Court of Appeals concluded that evidence of traffic-law violations and possible distracted driving created a jury issue on bad faith. The question before the Supreme Court of Georgia was whether violations of traffic laws, standing alone, are enough to support attorney fees for bad faith in the underlying transaction. The Court held that they are not and reversed. It explained that bad faith under OCGA 13-6-11 requires more than ordinary negligence or the mere breach of a public-safety statute; it demands evidence of intentional wrongdoing, conscious indifference, or comparable culpability tied to the transaction itself. Because the record showed, at most, negligent driving in an auto wreck, the claim for litigation expenses should not have gone to the jury on a bad-faith theory.

lucid-group-usa-inc-v-state-of-ga.title: Lucid Group USA, Inc. v. State of Ga.
lucid-group-usa-inc-v-state-of-ga.summary: Lucid sought to sell its electric vehicles directly to Georgia consumers, but the Department of Revenue denied its dealer-license application under statutes that require sales through independent franchised dealers and generally bar manufacturers from owning dealerships. Lucid sued, arguing that the direct-sales prohibition violated due process, equal protection, and Georgia?s ban on certain special laws. The Supreme Court of Georgia held that the trial court dismissed too much of the case too quickly. It vacated the ruling that a constitutional provision authorizing regulation of the motor-vehicle industry automatically defeated Lucid?s due-process and equal-protection claims, and it also remanded Lucid?s challenge to the 2015 amendment that created both the direct-sales prohibition and Tesla?s carveout. But the court affirmed dismissal of Lucid?s standalone attack on the Tesla exception itself, reasoning that Lucid lacked standing to challenge that carveout in isolation because its injury flowed from the broader direct-sales ban.

mahogany-v-state-366-ga-app-750.title: Mahogany v. State
tags: Georgia Street Gang Terrorism and Prevention Act
mahogany-v-state-366-ga-app-750.summary: Robert Jordan Mahogany was convicted of gang-act violations, armed robbery, aggravated assault, home invasion, and firearm offenses after an armed robbery in a private residence and the State tied him to the G-Shine Bloods through witness identifications, jail calls, expert testimony, and Facebook posts and videos. On appeal, he challenged the sufficiency of the evidence and argued that the trial court wrongly admitted gang evidence and hearsay, including testimony tied to a nonappearing victim's photo-lineup identification. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that the evidence was sufficient to prove both the robbery-related crimes and the gang counts, and that none of the evidentiary complaints required a new trial. The court's reasoning stressed the strong eyewitness identifications, Mahogany's own gang-themed social-media material, and the overwhelming proof of guilt, which rendered any arguable hearsay error harmless. It also declined to address a possible merger problem because the State had not cross-appealed and any correction would have hurt Mahogany.

martin-v-state-370-ga-app-275.title: Martin v. State
martin-v-state-370-ga-app-275.summary: Wayne Martin was convicted of possessing controlled substances found in a bedroom he shared with a probationer after her probation officer searched the residence and discovered drugs. The issues were whether the search violated the Fourth Amendment because the probationer had reported a different address and because Martin, a co-occupant, had not agreed to search conditions, and whether defense counsel was ineffective in litigating suppression. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that the probation officer had authority to search because the probationer was in fact living at the residence, her probation conditions allowed warrantless searches on reasonable cause, and Martin failed to show any unconstitutional seizure or search theory that would have changed the result. The court's reasoning focused on the probation officer's need to verify the probationer's actual residence, the evidence that she occupied the searched bedroom, and the lack of prejudice from any shortcomings in counsel's motion practice because the suppression motion would have failed anyway.

martin-argaw-v-state-369-ga-app-117.title: Martin-Argaw v. State
martin-argaw-v-state-369-ga-app-117.summary: On retrial, Tamarat Martin-Argaw was convicted of trying to hire a hit man to kill his estranged wife, her adult son, and a family friend while he was already jailed for an earlier violent attack on his wife. He argued that the trial court violated his confrontation rights by limiting evidence about a pending indictment against one of the State's witnesses. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It concluded that even if the restriction on cross-examination was error, the State proved beyond a reasonable doubt that the error was harmless. The court's reasoning emphasized that the challenged witness played only a minor and cumulative role, that other witnesses and recordings independently documented Martin-Argaw's murder-for-hire scheme, and that the jury heard overwhelming evidence from the undercover investigation, jail communications, and the history of Martin-Argaw's violence against the intended victim.

maynard-v-snapchat-inc-366-ga-app-507.title: Maynard v. Snapchat, Inc.
maynard-v-snapchat-inc-366-ga-app-507.summary: Wentworth Maynard was catastrophically injured when another driver allegedly used Snapchat's Speed Filter while racing past 100 miles per hour and rear-ended his car, and the Maynards sued Snapchat for negligent design. After the Supreme Court of Georgia had already revived the design-duty theory, the case returned to the Court of Appeals to decide whether the complaint still failed on proximate-cause grounds because the driver's reckless conduct was an intervening cause. The Court of Appeals reversed the dismissal. It held that the complaint plausibly alleged that Snapchat's design was a proximate cause of the crash because the Speed Filter allegedly encouraged dangerous speeding and the pleaded facts tied the driver's conduct directly to her desire to post a 100-mph snap. The court reasoned that intervening misconduct does not break causation at the pleading stage when that kind of misconduct is itself a foreseeable consequence of the product design, and the complaint alleged just that.

maynor-v-state-317-ga-492.title: Maynor v. State
maynor-v-state-317-ga-492.summary: Reginald Maynor admitted shooting Marti Stegall during a neighborhood Fourth of July gathering after a long-running dispute fueled by Stegall's affair with Maynor's partner, but he claimed he acted in self-defense. On appeal, he argued that the evidence was insufficient to disprove justification and that trial counsel was ineffective for failing to cross-examine a witness more effectively and for not seeking a mistrial after an opening-statement claim went unproven at trial. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed. It held that the jury was entitled to reject self-defense and that Maynor failed to show deficient performance or prejudice under Strickland. The reasoning pointed to evidence that Maynor had previously threatened to kill Stegall, to the jury's authority to resolve conflicting testimony about the shooting, and to the prosecutor's good-faith basis for the challenged opening remark, which meant a mistrial motion would have failed. With no established attorney error, the cumulative-prejudice claim collapsed as well.

mcbrayer-v-scarbrough-317-ga-387.title: McBrayer v. Scarbrough
tags: Negligent Use of a Covered Motor Vehicle
mcbrayer-v-scarbrough-317-ga-387.summary: Sherrie McBrayer sued the Tift County sheriff for wrongful death after her husband died while restrained in the back seat of a patrol car, and the lower courts held the sheriff immune because the patrol car supposedly was not being actively used as a vehicle when the injury arose. The Supreme Court of Georgia took the case to decide what counts as the "use" of a covered motor vehicle under Georgia's statutory waiver of sovereign immunity for motor-vehicle claims. It reversed and remanded. The Court held that the Court of Appeals had read extra words into the statute by requiring the vehicle to be in active use and being used specifically as a vehicle. The reasoning was straightforward textualism: the statutes waive immunity for negligent use of a covered motor vehicle, and loading, restraining, or otherwise employing a patrol car in law-enforcement custody can qualify without the engine running or transport already underway. The case was sent back for the lower court to apply the correct understanding of "use."

mcelrath-v-georgia.title: McElrath v. Georgia
tags: Supreme Court of the United States
mcelrath-v-georgia.summary: Damian McElrath was found not guilty by reason of insanity on a malice-murder count but guilty but mentally ill on felony-murder and aggravated-assault counts arising from the same killing of his mother, and Georgia courts treated those verdicts as repugnant and authorized a full retrial. The issue before the Supreme Court of the United States was whether the Double Jeopardy Clause still barred retrial on the count that produced the insanity acquittal. The Court unanimously said yes and reversed. It held that a verdict of not guilty by reason of insanity is an acquittal for double-jeopardy purposes, even if other verdicts in the same case appear logically inconsistent. The reasoning rejected judicial second-guessing of what the jury meant: once the jury returned and the trial court accepted an acquittal on the malice-murder count, the Constitution barred the State from trying McElrath again on that charge.

mcwhirter-v-clinkscales.title: McWhirter v. Clinkscales
tags: General Grounds Motion for New Trial
mcwhirter-v-clinkscales.summary: The parents of a 15-month-old boy who died after a button battery eroded through his esophagus won a malpractice verdict against the child?s pediatrician and her practice, alleging the doctor should have considered a swallowed foreign object and ordered imaging earlier. On appeal, the defendants challenged the admission of a causation expert, the denial of a new trial on the general grounds, and the refusal to apply a statutory cap on noneconomic damages. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that the expert testimony was admissible for the jury to weigh, that the record did not rebut the usual presumption that the trial judge properly exercised discretion in denying the general-grounds motion, and that the appellate court could not reach the damages-cap issue because the trial court never distinctly ruled on the constitutional question the defendants chose to press on appeal.

metropolitan-atlanta-rapid-transit-auth-.title: Metropolitan Atlanta Rapid Transit Auth. v. Brown
metropolitan-atlanta-rapid-transit-auth-.summary: Norman Brown sued MARTA after he fell while boarding a train at Georgia State station and claimed the transit authority?s negligence caused his injuries. On interlocutory appeal, the issue was whether Brown had enough evidence on the essential elements of negligence to survive summary judgment. The Court of Appeals of Georgia held that he did not and reversed the trial court. The court reasoned that even viewing the record favorably to Brown, the proof did not create a genuine jury question on the element needed to impose liability on MARTA as a common carrier in these circumstances. Without evidence tying the fall to actionable negligence rather than speculation, the case could not proceed.

mohr-v-state-370-ga-app-194.title: Mohr v. State
mohr-v-state-370-ga-app-194.summary: Stuart Mohr was convicted of aggravated assault after brutally attacking a patron inside his bar, and the State's proof included surveillance footage obtained from the bar's recording system. On appeal, he argued that the footage should have been suppressed because officers executed the search warrant 13 days after it was issued, that the trial court wrongly excluded testimony, and that trial counsel was ineffective. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that even if the delayed warrant execution violated the ten-day statute, the delay was at most a technical irregularity that did not affect Mohr's substantial rights because officers already lawfully possessed the equipment, Mohr voluntarily supplied the password, and the later review of the contents did not create a constitutional problem. That same lack of suppression prejudice doomed the ineffective-assistance claim, and the remaining evidentiary arguments did not justify a new trial.

montgomery-v-state-315-ga-467.title: Montgomery v. State
montgomery-v-state-315-ga-467.summary: Gregory Montgomery was convicted of malice murder and related crimes after a woman lured Justuss Rogers to a cul-de-sac so he could be robbed, and Montgomery approached the stopped Camaro and opened fire. On appeal, he argued that the trial court botched a jury recharge during deliberations and should have granted a new trial under the general-grounds or thirteenth-juror standard. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed. It held that the recharge was not erroneous and that the appellate court could not revisit the trial judge's discretionary weighing of the evidence so long as the judge actually applied the thirteenth-juror standard. The reasoning emphasized that the trial court did use the correct general-grounds framework and that the trial evidence, including digital evidence and physical items found near the scene, was constitutionally sufficient to support Montgomery's convictions as the shooter and participant in the planned robbery.

munro-v-department-of-transportation.title: Munro v. Department of Transportation
munro-v-department-of-transportation.summary: Ashleigh Munro's parents sued the Georgia Department of Transportation after a fatal collision at a state-road intersection, alleging negligent design and negligent inspection and maintenance of the intersection. The question was whether the Georgia Tort Claims Act waived sovereign immunity for either theory. The Court of Appeals of Georgia split the claims. It held that the plaintiffs failed to show a waiver for the negligent-design theory, but did show a waiver for the negligent-inspection claim tied to the DOT's alleged failure to inspect and maintain the intersection by removing hazardous vegetation. The reasoning treated design and inspection as distinct functions under the Tort Claims Act: design fell within the immunity-protected planning sphere, while inspection and maintenance of physical roadway conditions did not, at least on the pleadings and arguments the DOT actually made in the case.

myfamilydoc-llc-v-johnston-366-ga-app-45.title: MyFamilyDoc, LLC v. Johnston
myfamilydoc-llc-v-johnston-366-ga-app-45.summary: Wayne Johnston's estate sued his doctor, a nurse practitioner, and their practice after no one timely reviewed lab results showing severe anemia that allegedly required emergency treatment before Johnston died. On interlocutory review, the issues were whether nurse practitioner Mary Chastain owed Johnston a duty of care and whether the plaintiff's causation expert should have been excluded. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed the denial of summary judgment for Chastain but otherwise affirmed. It held that Chastain pointed to undisputed evidence showing she owed no duty to Johnston in the circumstances presented, yet the trial court acted within its discretion in admitting the expert causation testimony against the remaining defendants. The reasoning distinguished between who actually bore responsibility for reviewing the results and whether the expert used a sufficiently reliable medical methodology in tying Johnston's untreated severe anemia to his death.

myrick-v-fulton-cnty-69-f-4th-1277.title: Myrick v. Fulton Cnty.
myrick-v-fulton-cnty-69-f-4th-1277.summary: The family of Antonio May sued after May died in the Fulton County Jail following methamphetamine intoxication, mental-health distress, a jail intake process, and a later altercation with officers. The appeal asked whether claims against the sheriff, jail officers, a jail medical employee, and the private medical contractor NaphCare were properly dismissed or resolved on summary judgment. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed most of the district court's rulings but revived the case against NaphCare. It held that the record did not support liability against Sheriff Jackson, the officers, or employee Travis Williams, but that the plaintiffs' expert evidence created a genuine issue of fact on whether NaphCare's failure to screen, observe, classify, and treat May properly proximately caused his death. The reasoning emphasized that the NaphCare claim was not just about sedation in the abstract; it was about a broader alleged failure to follow through medically with an obviously distressed detainee, and a jury could credit that theory even with the later officer encounter in the chain of events.

nbcuniversal-media-llc-v-walker.title: NBCUniversal Media, LLC v. Walker
nbcuniversal-media-llc-v-walker.summary: NBCUniversal was hit with a continuing-garnishment default judgment exceeding $500,000 after it failed to answer on time, even though it contended that a timely answer would have shown it owed less than $1,000. NBCUniversal moved to set the judgment aside and argued that Georgia?s garnishment-default statute was unconstitutional under due process and the Georgia Excessive Fines Clause. The Supreme Court of Georgia did not reach those merits. Instead, it held that the trial court?s summary denial did not distinctly or necessarily decide the constitutional questions, because the motion could have been denied on the separate statutory ground that no nonamendable defect appeared on the face of the record. Without a clear ruling on the constitutional issues, the Supreme Court lacked jurisdiction, so it vacated its earlier grant of discretionary review, transferred the application to the Court of Appeals, and dismissed the direct appeal.

norris-v-atlanta-braves-inc.title: Norris v. Atlanta Braves, Inc.
norris-v-atlanta-braves-inc.summary: Mayra Norris was struck in the eye by a baseball allegedly thrown overhand and with force by Braves outfielder Jorge Soler between innings at a World Series game, and she and her husband sued both Soler and the Braves. The trial court dismissed the case at the pleading stage under the Baseball Rule, reasoning that souvenir balls thrown into the crowd are inherent risks of sitting in unprotected seats. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed. It held that the complaint did not establish with certainty that the Baseball Rule barred every possible theory of recovery, especially given the allegations that the throw happened while the game was not in play and was neither an ordinary game action nor a routine warmup. Because the pleadings still left room for proof on negligence, premises liability, vicarious liability, and the derivative claims, dismissal was premature.

owens-v-state-2026-ga-lexis-62.title: Owens v. State
owens-v-state-2026-ga-lexis-62.summary: Maria Owens was retried for the death of an eleven-month-old child after her original convictions had been reversed under the since-abandoned rule treating certain verdicts with different mental states as mutually exclusive. At the retrial, however, the jury was again instructed that it could not convict her of both involuntary manslaughter and the intent-based offenses of aggravated assault and first-degree child cruelty. The issue on appeal was whether that instruction was erroneous after Georgia had already rejected the old mutually-exclusive-mental-states rule. The Supreme Court of Georgia held that it was, and that the error was harmful as to the homicide counts. The governing rule is that negligence-based and intent-based offenses are not categorically incompatible, so a trial court may not force the jury to choose between them on that discredited theory. Because the bad instruction tainted the felony-murder verdict, the Court reversed that conviction, allowed a retrial because the evidence was constitutionally sufficient, left the child-cruelty conviction intact, and vacated that sentence because the final sentencing package will depend on how the homicide charges are resolved.

owens-v-state.title: Owens v. State
owens-v-state.summary: Christopher Owens was convicted after a brutal bar attack in which members of a motorcycle club beat and stabbed a patron, leaving multiple violent-injury counts and knife-possession charges. On appeal from the denial of his motion for new trial, Owens challenged the sufficiency of the evidence on several counts, asserted ineffective assistance, and argued that the trial court mishandled merger at sentencing. The Court of Appeals of Georgia largely upheld the verdicts but found part of the sentencing analysis legally wrong. It held that one aggravated-assault count should have merged into aggravated battery rather than the other way around, and that the trial court erred by refusing to analyze whether the separate aggravated-battery counts merged under the proper unit-of-prosecution framework. The court therefore affirmed in part, vacated in part, and remanded for a fresh merger analysis while leaving the underlying guilty findings otherwise intact.

owens-v-state-303-ga-254.title: Owens v. State
owens-v-state-303-ga-254.summary: Margie Owens was convicted after shooting her husband, but the jury returned a voluntary-manslaughter verdict as a lesser offense of malice murder while the trial court sentenced her for felony murder based on aggravated assault. The issues were whether counsel was ineffective and whether sentencing Owens for felony murder violated Georgia's modified merger rule under Edge v. State when the evidence supported a heat-of-passion voluntary-manslaughter finding. The Supreme Court of Georgia rejected the ineffective-assistance claims but vacated the felony-murder conviction and remanded for entry of a voluntary-manslaughter conviction and sentence. The court held that the Edge rule barred the felony-murder sentence on these facts. It also used the case to confront the extraordinary 19-year delay in resolving the motion for new trial, directing the superior-court system to propose a uniform rule aimed at preventing unjustified post-trial, pre-appeal delays in criminal cases.

patrick-v-kingston-370-ga-app-570.title: Patrick v. Kingston
patrick-v-kingston-370-ga-app-570.summary: After Jennifer Patrick died in a wrong-way collision, her family sent the tortfeasor's insurer a time-limited settlement demand that had to be accepted unequivocally and without any variance. The trial court enforced a settlement, but the issue on appeal was whether the insurer's response actually matched the offer or instead altered a material term. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed. It held that there was no binding settlement because the insurer's chosen payment method and related terms did not mirror the offer exactly, which meant the purported acceptance operated as a counteroffer rather than a contract. The reasoning applied straightforward contract principles to Georgia's pre-suit settlement-demand statute: when an offer demands exact acceptance, even a single material variance defeats formation, so the court did not need to reach the family's additional argument that the trial court exceeded its jurisdiction by ordering specific performance from the insurer.

peachstate-concessionaires-inc-v-bryant.title: Peachstate Concessionaires, Inc. v. Bryant
peachstate-concessionaires-inc-v-bryant.summary: A Dunkin' Donuts cashier sued her employer after a customer returned to the store, argued with her over an earlier drive-through exchange, and stabbed her during the confrontation. The trial court allowed her premises-liability and negligent-security theories to proceed, concluding that it could not decide as a matter of law whether the injury arose out of her employment and was therefore barred by the Workers' Compensation Act's exclusive-remedy provision. The Court of Appeals reversed. It held that the assault stemmed directly from Bryant's performance of her cashier duties and from a dispute generated by that work interaction, making the risk reasonably incident to the employment relationship. The governing rule is that an employee's tort suit against the employer is barred when the injury both occurs in the course of employment and arises out of risks reasonably connected to the job, even when the immediate harm is inflicted by a third-party assailant. Applying that rule, the court directed entry of summary judgment for the employer on the remaining tort claims.

perez-v-bush-2025-ga-state-lexis-860.title: Perez v. Bush
perez-v-bush-2025-ga-state-lexis-860.summary: This State Court order arose from a vehicle rollover collision in Forsyth County after Carl Bush allegedly failed to yield while pulling out of a shopping-center exit. Bush moved for partial summary judgment on punitive damages and attorney fees, and the plaintiff withdrew the punitive-damages claim, leaving only OCGA 13-6-11 in dispute. The issue was whether Bush's traffic citation and litigation position created enough evidence of bad faith, stubborn litigiousness, or unnecessary trouble and expense to let attorney fees go forward. The court held that they did not and granted partial summary judgment for Bush on the fee claim. It reasoned that the record showed, at most, ordinary negligence rather than conscious wrongdoing, and that the parties still had a bona fide controversy over liability, causation, and damages. Because OCGA 13-6-11 does not authorize fees based merely on a routine negligence dispute and contested liability defenses, the fee request failed as a matter of law.

perrault-v-state-316-ga-241.title: Perrault v. State
perrault-v-state-316-ga-241.summary: Michael Seth Perrault, then a police officer, was convicted of murdering his wife Amanda days after she reported that he had choked and assaulted her during a domestic-violence incident. On appeal, he challenged the sufficiency of the evidence, argued that pretrial publicity required a change of venue, and sought a new trial on cumulative-error grounds. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed. It held that the evidence authorized the jury to find Perrault guilty, that he did not show the level of prejudice necessary to require transfer to another venue, and that his cumulative-error argument failed because he did not establish any actual trial errors to aggregate. The court's reasoning relied on the evidence surrounding the domestic-abuse episode, the timeline and security-record evidence around Amanda's death, and the settled rule that cumulative prejudice cannot be built from vague complaints or non-errors.

perry-v-state-366-ga-app-341.title: Perry v. State
perry-v-state-366-ga-app-341.summary: Shalynda Perry was convicted of aggravated assault and aggravated battery after a drunken confrontation near a fire barrel left the victim severely burned, and Perry claimed she acted in self-defense after the victim came at her with a bottle. The appeal focused on two questions: whether the evidence supported the aggravated-battery conviction and whether the jury was properly instructed on justification. The Court of Appeals of Georgia held that the evidence was sufficient but still reversed. It ruled that the trial court plainly erred by failing to tell the jury that the State had the burden to disprove Perry's justification defense beyond a reasonable doubt. The reasoning turned on the fact that self-defense was the central disputed issue at trial and the missing burden instruction could have led jurors to think Perry had to prove justification herself, which created a substantial risk of an unreliable verdict.

pierce-v-banks-368-ga-app-496.title: Pierce v. Banks
pierce-v-banks-368-ga-app-496.summary: After a car crash, Aaron Pierce sent a detailed pre-suit settlement demand requiring Trexis to accept the offer exactly, including strict terms about payment timing and prohibiting any extra conditions or expirations. Trexis responded with what it called an acceptance, but the settlement check said it would be void after 180 days and was delivered with materials Pierce argued varied from the offer. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed the order enforcing settlement. It held that no settlement contract was formed because the insurer's response was not unconditional and identical to the offer, so it operated as a counteroffer rather than an acceptance. The court's reasoning applied basic contract law to the settlement-demand setting: when the offeror requires exact compliance, even a seemingly small added restriction like a 180-day void notation defeats the meeting of the minds necessary to bind the parties.

pittman-v-state-366-ga-app-372.title: Pittman v. State
pittman-v-state-366-ga-app-372.summary: Matthew Pittman originally received a misdemeanor sentence that included 180 days in a probation detention center, but that part of the sentence was void because only certain felony offenders are eligible for that placement. When the trial court later replaced the PDC time with county-jail time, Pittman argued the court lacked authority to increase the confinement after he had already begun serving the sentence and that resentencing by emailed order violated his right to be present. The Court of Appeals of Georgia rejected his void-sentence and double-jeopardy arguments but still vacated and remanded. It held that the trial court had discretion to correct the void sentence and impose jail time, yet it had to reimpose that modified sentence in open court with Pittman present because resentencing is a critical stage of the criminal case. The reasoning distinguishes between the power to fix an illegal sentence and the separate procedural requirement that the correction occur in the defendant's presence.

powell-v-state-318-ga-875.title: Powell v. State
powell-v-state-318-ga-875.summary: Former Glynn County police leaders John Powell and Brian Scott were indicted for violating their oaths of office based on allegations that they failed to investigate officer misconduct in ways the State claimed amounted to Brady violations. The issue on appeal was whether the indictment, taking its factual allegations as true, actually charged a crime and could survive general demurrer. The Supreme Court of Georgia reversed. It held that the indictment was legally insufficient because the conduct alleged did not amount to Brady violations and therefore did not support the oath-of-office charges as pled. The court's reasoning was blunt: even assuming Brady could ever serve as the predicate for such a prosecution, these counts described administrative failures that, as alleged, did not satisfy Brady, so the defendants could admit every fact in the indictment and still be innocent of the charged offenses.

priester-v-state-316-ga-133.title: Priester v. State
priester-v-state-316-ga-133.summary: Joseph Priester was convicted of murdering a gas-station customer after surveillance footage showed the shooter ambushing the victim from a green Avalanche, and the State also introduced evidence that Priester had committed an armed robbery and shooting the day before. On appeal, Priester argued that the earlier incident should not have come in under Rule 404 (b) and that the jury should not have been instructed it could use that evidence for multiple purposes. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed. It held that no reversible error occurred because the trial evidence strongly identified Priester as the shooter and any problem with the limiting instruction did not likely affect the verdict. The court's reasoning emphasized the surveillance footage, proof that Priester possessed the distinctive Avalanche, and cell-site data placing him near the scene, which made the State's case strong enough that any instructional overbreadth about the other-act evidence was harmless.

priester-v-turner-370-ga-app-269.title: Priester v. Turner
priester-v-turner-370-ga-app-269.summary: Shelly Priester sued for loss of consortium after her husband was injured in a car wreck, but the trial court held that her claim was barred because her husband's separate renewal action had previously been dismissed with prejudice for lack of service and discovery violations. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed. It held that, although a consortium claim is derivative in the sense that it arises from injury to the other spouse, it remains an independent cause of action personal to the consortium claimant and was not automatically barred by the procedural dismissal of the husband's separately filed lawsuit. The reasoning distinguished cases where the injured spouse never had any viable tort claim at all from this case, where the husband's claim failed for procedural reasons rather than because the underlying tort claim never existed. Under Stapleton, that difference matters, and Shelly Priester's separate action could proceed.

prodigies-child-care-mgmt-llc-v-cotton.title: Prodigies Child Care Mgmt., LLC v. Cotton
prodigies-child-care-mgmt-llc-v-cotton.summary: Andrea Cotton sued a daycare operator after one of its employees crossed the median during her lunch-break return drive and crashed into Cotton while scrolling through her phone to call her manager about being late. The Supreme Court of Georgia used the case to decide how Georgia courts should analyze employer liability when an employee causes a wreck while commuting or returning from lunch but is also doing something arguably work-related. The Court vacated the Court of Appeals decision and remanded. It held that Georgia does not recognize a free-standing "special circumstances exception" to the going-and-coming rule; the only real question is the ordinary respondeat-superior inquiry of whether the employee was acting in furtherance of the employer's business and within the scope of employment at the moment of the tort. The reasoning rejected the Court of Appeals' multi-factor exception framework and required the lower court to apply the standard scope-and-furtherance analysis instead.

quint-v-state-367-ga-app-339.title: Quint v. State
quint-v-state-367-ga-app-339.summary: Tracie Quint crashed her car, showed signs of intoxication at the scene, and refused a state-administered blood test after an officer told her he was placing her under arrest for DUI and read the implied-consent notice. On appeal, Quint argued that her refusal should have been suppressed because she was not actually under arrest before the notice was read. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that the implied-consent procedure was properly triggered because a reasonable person in Quint's position would have understood she was under arrest and not free to leave when the officer read the notice. The reasoning relied on the surrounding circumstances, including officers telling Quint she could go to the hospital or to jail, her inability to simply go home, and the officer's explicit statement that he was arresting her for DUI immediately before reading the notice.

raffensperger-v-jackson-316-ga-383.title: Raffensperger v. Jackson
raffensperger-v-jackson-316-ga-383.summary: Mary Jackson and ROSE challenged Georgia's Lactation Consultant Practice Act, which allowed only providers holding the private IBCLC credential to obtain a state license to provide paid lactation care. After remand from an earlier dismissal, the case returned to the Supreme Court of Georgia on cross-motions for summary judgment over whether the Act violated the Georgia Constitution. The Court held that the Act was unconstitutional on due-process grounds and reversed the trial court's ruling for the Secretary of State. It concluded that the licensing scheme lacked a real and substantial relation to public health, safety, or welfare because the record showed no evidence of harm from non-IBCLC providers and substantial evidence that non-IBCLC lactation support was safe and beneficial. Because that due-process ruling disposed of the case, the Court vacated the equal-protection ruling without reaching it.

ramos-v-owens-366-ga-app-216.title: Ramos v. Owens
ramos-v-owens-366-ga-app-216.summary: Michael Ramos sued the Cobb County Sheriff in his official capacity after injuring his knee while performing detention-center landscaping work without what he said was proper footwear. The issue was whether Ramos had carried his burden to show a waiver of sovereign immunity, particularly through alleged liability insurance. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed dismissal for lack of subject-matter jurisdiction. It held that Ramos offered only conclusory assertions about insurance and cited no statute or policy details showing an actual waiver of the sheriff's sovereign immunity. The reasoning emphasized that sovereign immunity is a jurisdictional bar, the plaintiff bears the burden of pleading and proving a specific waiver, and Ramos could not shift that burden by invoking failure-to-state-a-claim standards or asking for discovery for the first time on appeal.

randolph-v-state-317-ga-146.title: Randolph v. State
randolph-v-state-317-ga-146.summary: James Randolph was convicted of malice murder, armed robbery, and related crimes stemming from a 2000 hotel robbery that ended with the shooting death of night manager Rodney Castlin. On appeal, he argued that the State failed to corroborate its accomplice witness and that the trial court wrongly admitted two other-acts incidents under Rule 404 (b). The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed. It held that the accomplice's testimony was sufficiently corroborated by independent evidence, that one prior armed-robbery incident was properly admitted, and that any error in admitting a later home-burglary incident was harmless. The court's reasoning pointed to corroborating physical and testimonial evidence, including Randolph's fingerprint at the scene, and concluded that the strength of the State's case and the trial court's limiting instructions made it highly unlikely that any Rule 404 (b) overreach changed the outcome.

rivera-v-state-317-ga-398.title: Rivera v. State
rivera-v-state-317-ga-398.summary: More than two decades after Bridgett Parker was raped and killed, new DNA testing matched Octavious Rivera to biological evidence from her sexual-assault kit, and he was convicted of felony murder and rape. On appeal, Rivera argued that the indictment failed to handle the statute of limitation properly on the rape and aggravated-assault counts and that the trial court wrongly admitted other-acts evidence. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed the felony-murder conviction but reversed the rape conviction and remanded for resentencing. It held that the rape count could not stand under the applicable statute-of-limitation analysis, while any error in admitting the challenged other-acts evidence was harmless in light of the strong evidence of guilt on the homicide-related charges. The reasoning rested on the powerful DNA proof, corroborating circumstantial evidence tying Rivera to the crime scene, and the court's conclusion that the extrinsic-act evidence did not materially drive the jury's verdict on the surviving conviction.

robertson-v-state-366-ga-app-623.title: Robertson v. State
robertson-v-state-366-ga-app-623.summary: Norman Robertson was convicted after a bench trial of DUI, failure to obey a traffic control device, and failure to maintain lane, but he challenged the denial of his motion to suppress evidence that he refused roadside field-sobriety evaluations. The issue was whether Georgia?s constitutional protection against self-incrimination barred the State from using his refusal at trial. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed the traffic convictions but vacated the DUI judgment and remanded. It held that, under intervening Supreme Court of Georgia precedent, the refusal evidence had to be reconsidered, and the DUI conviction could not stand as entered. The reasoning emphasized that the State relied on the refusal as part of its DUI proof and that Robertson?s jury-trial waiver was tied to the suppression ruling, making harmless-error treatment inappropriate on this record.

rodriguez-v-state-farm-mut-auto-ins-co-3.title: Rodriguez v. State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co.
rodriguez-v-state-farm-mut-auto-ins-co-3.summary: Moises Rodriguez sued State Farm for uninsured-motorist benefits after he could not perfect service on the at-fault driver within the limitations period despite efforts to locate her. The issue was whether those efforts were enough to preserve a direct action against the UM carrier under OCGA ? 33-7-11. The Court of Appeals of Georgia held that they were not and affirmed dismissal. The court reasoned that the statute imposes specific service-related conditions before a UM carrier can remain in the case when the uninsured motorist is not served, and Rodriguez?s showing did not satisfy those statutory requirements. Diligent effort alone was not enough where the statutory gateway had not been met.

roman-v-state.title: Roman v. State
roman-v-state.summary: Multiple defendants charged in the Fulton County 2020 election-interference prosecution sought dismissal of the indictment and disqualification of District Attorney Fani Willis and her office after the trial court found a significant appearance of impropriety but allowed the prosecution to continue if special prosecutor Nathan Wade withdrew. The Court of Appeals of Georgia held that the trial court chose the wrong remedy. It reversed the denial of the motion to disqualify but affirmed the refusal to dismiss the indictment. The court reasoned that, given the trial court?s unchallenged finding of a significant appearance of impropriety tied to the district attorney?s own conduct, allowing the office to remain in the case after Wade?s withdrawal did not cure the problem. But the court found no basis to throw out the indictment itself, so the prosecution survived even though the district attorney?s office could not remain on it.

rose-v-sec-y-87-f-4th-469.title: Rose v. Sec'y
rose-v-sec-y-87-f-4th-469.summary: Black Georgia voters challenged the statewide at-large election system for the Georgia Public Service Commission, arguing that it diluted Black voting strength in violation of Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act and that single-member districts were the proper fix. The district court agreed, but the Eleventh Circuit reversed. It held that the plaintiffs failed to propose a viable remedy because replacing statewide PSC elections with single-member districts would fundamentally alter Georgia?s chosen structure for a constitutionally created statewide regulatory body. The court?s reasoning leaned on federalism, the statewide nature of the Commission?s duties, and its view that the plaintiffs could not satisfy the first Gingles precondition without a legally permissible remedy that fit the office they were challenging.

rosenbaum-v-state.title: Rosenbaum v. State
rosenbaum-v-state.summary: Jennifer Rosenbaum was convicted of felony murder and multiple assaultive and child-cruelty counts arising from the fatal abuse of a two-year-old foster child and the abuse of the child?s older sister. On appeal, she argued that counsel was ineffective for not seeking a justification charge and that counsel labored under an actual conflict because of joint defense issues with her husband?s case. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed. It held that Rosenbaum knowingly, voluntarily, and intelligently waived any conflict-free-counsel objection and therefore could not obtain relief on that ground, and the remaining ineffective-assistance arguments failed as well. The reasoning focused on the extensive medical evidence disproving Rosenbaum?s choking-and-rescue account, the trial court?s handling of the conflict issue, and Rosenbaum?s documented waiver after consulting independent counsel about the risks of joint representation.

royal-v-state-farm-mut-auto-ins-co-366-g.title: Royal v. State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co.
royal-v-state-farm-mut-auto-ins-co-366-g.summary: Lance Royal tried to stack uninsured-motorist coverage across more than twenty State Farm policies issued to his father after Royal was injured while driving his employer?s van. The question was whether Royal qualified under those other policies as an insured who could access and combine their UM coverage. The Court of Appeals of Georgia held that he could not, except under the policy covering the vehicle he occupied. The court reasoned that Royal was neither a named insured nor a resident relative under the remaining policies, and being listed as a driver on renewal paperwork did not expand the contracts? actual coverage terms. With the policy language and statute aligned against broader stacking, summary judgment was proper.

savannah-state-univ-found-inc-v-lewis-37.title: Savannah State Univ. Found., Inc. v. Lewis
savannah-state-univ-found-inc-v-lewis-37.summary: The estate of a nonstudent who was shot while visiting friends in Savannah State student housing sued the university foundation entities tied to the real-estate financing structure for the complex. The issue was whether those entities possessed or controlled the property closely enough to face premises-liability or nuisance exposure. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed the denial of summary judgment. It held that the LLC was an out-of-possession landlord under the governing lease structure and that the Foundation, as the LLC?s member, exercised no greater control. The reasoning relied heavily on lease documents and recent precedent involving nearly identical university-foundation arrangements, concluding that neither defendant retained the kind of possession or control necessary to support premises-liability or nuisance claims for the shooting.

sheffield-v-jekyll-island-state-park-aut.title: Sheffield v. Jekyll Island State Park Authority
sheffield-v-jekyll-island-state-park-aut.summary: Tanya Sheffield injured her foot, knee, and hip at a Jekyll Island water park when she came out of a slide and said her foot struck something hard in the landing area, though she could not identify exactly what. The issue was whether she had evidence of a hazardous condition and the park?s superior knowledge of it. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed summary judgment for the park authority. It held that Sheffield could not recover because she could not identify the cause of her injury or prove that the defendant knew of any dangerous condition related to the slide exit or water level. The court?s reasoning emphasized the absence of evidence of a defect, the lack of proof of the park?s superior knowledge, and inspection records showing no unsafe condition, which made it unnecessary to resolve the park?s alternative assumption-of-risk defense.

shingler-v-ga-farm-bureau-mut-ins-co-369.title: Shingler v. Ga. Farm Bureau Mut. Ins. Co.
shingler-v-ga-farm-bureau-mut-ins-co-369.summary: After Hurricane Michael damaged Ronald Shingler?s farm, Georgia Farm Bureau paid some claims but denied others, and Shingler later sued for breach of contract and bad faith. The insurer argued that the suit was barred by the policy?s one-year limitation period for non-fire losses. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed summary judgment for the insurer. It held that the one-year suit limitation was enforceable, that the policy language was not ambiguous in the way Shingler claimed, and that the insurer?s partial payments and ongoing communications did not amount to a waiver. The reasoning stressed that the insurer repeatedly denied portions of the claim, expressly reserved all defenses, and even reminded Shingler of the limitation provision, so there was no evidence that he had been lulled into delaying suit.

simmons-v-bates-366-ga-app-410.title: Simmons v. Bates
simmons-v-bates-366-ga-app-410.summary: After a fatal motor-vehicle collision, the decedent?s survivors sent a highly detailed time-limited settlement demand that required unequivocal acceptance by two separate insurers. Only one insurer responded, and the defendant argued that a binding settlement nonetheless existed. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed the denial of the motion to enforce settlement. It held that no settlement contract formed because the offer was expressly contingent on acceptance by both insurers and that condition never occurred. The reasoning underscored that the claimants were free to be the masters of their own offer, however convoluted the demand may have been, and contract law did not permit the court to rewrite the offer into something more reasonable or easier to accept.

simmons-v-solo-cup-operating-corporation.title: Simmons v. Solo Cup Operating Corporation
simmons-v-solo-cup-operating-corporation.summary: Deborah Simmons sued Solo after her son, a Solo employee, was shot and killed at work by a coworker who believed he had stolen money from a lost bag found in the plant restroom. The issue was whether the Workers? Compensation Act provided the exclusive remedy and barred the wrongful-death suit. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed the dismissal. It held that the death did not arise out of employment because the shooting stemmed from a personal dispute between the assailant and the decedent, not from a work-created risk. The reasoning distinguished positional-risk cases involving neutral workplace dangers and concluded that Andrews was targeted for reasons personal to him, which placed the case outside workers? compensation exclusivity and allowed the civil action to proceed.

simms-v-state-2026-ga-lexis-101.title: Simms v. State
simms-v-state-2026-ga-lexis-101.summary: Michael Eric Simms appealed his felony-murder conviction after learning that a seated juror was not a United States citizen, even though the juror had said during voir dire that he was from Mexico and had truthfully disclosed his non-citizenship on the court?s questionnaire. The issue was whether Simms waived that juror-qualification claim by failing to object at trial. The Supreme Court of Georgia vacated the denial of Simms?s motion for new trial and remanded. The court held that silence alone does not waive an objection to an ineligible juror; the trial court had to decide whether Simms or his counsel actually knew of the juror?s ineligibility or could have discovered it through ordinary diligence. Because the trial court stopped at the absence of a trial objection and never applied that fuller waiver standard, the preservation ruling could not stand.

smalley-v-state-371-ga-app-253.title: Smalley v. State
smalley-v-state-371-ga-app-253.summary: Kentavius Smalley, a first-time felony offender, received consecutive straight-probation sentences after a negotiated guilty plea, but the trial court refused to include a behavioral incentive date in the sentencing order. The issue was whether OCGA ? 17-10-1 required a BID for a defendant like Smalley. The Court of Appeals of Georgia vacated and remanded. It held that the statute?s plain language required the trial court to include a BID because Smalley was a first-time felony offender convicted of felonies and given a qualifying probationary sentence. The reasoning rejected the State?s narrower reading of the statute and applied the rule that ambiguous criminal statutes are construed in the defendant?s favor if necessary, though the court found the ordinary text already pointed to Smalley?s interpretation.

smith-v-state.title: Smith v. State
smith-v-state.summary: Danyel Smith was convicted in 2003 of felony murder in the death of his two-month-old son based on a Shaken Baby Syndrome theory. Years later, Smith filed an extraordinary motion for new trial supported by new expert analysis and modern medical literature contending that the infant?s injuries were more consistent with birth trauma, seizures, and other non-abuse explanations than with shaking. After an evidentiary hearing, the trial court denied relief again. The Supreme Court of Georgia vacated that ruling and remanded. It held that the trial court used the wrong framework when assessing materiality: instead of asking how a reasonable jury would likely weigh the new expert evidence against the trial evidence, the court largely decided for itself which experts it believed. Because newly discovered expert opinion must be evaluated through the probable-jury-impact framework established in Georgia new-trial law, the order could not stand.

smoot-lee-v-corizon-health-inc-366-ga-ap.title: Smoot-Lee v. Corizon Health, Inc.
smoot-lee-v-corizon-health-inc-366-ga-ap.summary: A detention-center employee sued Corizon after an inmate with apparent mental-health problems attacked her, claiming Corizon negligently failed to provide psychiatric or medical treatment that would have prevented the assault. The issue was whether the jail?s medical contractor owed a duty to the employee under Georgia law based on its relationship with the inmate. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed summary judgment for Corizon. It held that Corizon did not exercise the kind of control over the inmate necessary to create liability to third parties because custody and security decisions remained with the sheriff?s office, not the medical vendor. The reasoning relied on Georgia precedent limiting third-party liability for mental-health providers to situations involving actual control or confinement and rejected the argument that Corizon?s alleged ability to recommend involuntary measures was enough by itself.

soundara-v-amb-sports-entertainment-371-.title: Soundara v. AMB Sports & Entertainment
soundara-v-amb-sports-entertainment-371-.summary: Lynda Soundara was injured during a fight at Mercedes-Benz Stadium and sued the stadium entities, their security contractor, and the two spectators involved in the brawl. The Court of Appeals split the claims. It affirmed summary judgment for the stadium defendants and the security company on negligence-based theories, holding that Soundara assumed the risk when she voluntarily inserted herself into an active altercation. But it reversed summary judgment for the two individual fighters because assumption of the risk is not a defense to intentional torts like assault and battery. The reasoning drew a sharp line between negligence claims tied to voluntary exposure to danger and intentional-tort claims based on direct violent conduct.

spratlin-v-state-366-ga-app-607.title: Spratlin v. State
spratlin-v-state-366-ga-app-607.summary: Dusty Spratlin was convicted of criminal trespass after the alleged victim, who later died of unrelated causes, reported during a 911 call and to responding officers that he had shot her van windshield with a BB gun. The issue was whether those out-of-court statements could be admitted without first addressing the Sixth Amendment confrontation problem. The Court of Appeals of Georgia vacated and remanded. It held that the trial court erred by jumping straight to a hearsay exception analysis and failing to first determine under Crawford whether the statements were testimonial. The reasoning stressed that a court must separately decide the Confrontation Clause question before it can rely on hearsay exceptions like excited utterance, so the conviction could not stand on the record as analyzed.

starship-enters-of-atlanta-inc-v-gwinnet.title: Starship Enters. of Atlanta, Inc. v. Gwinnett County
starship-enters-of-atlanta-inc-v-gwinnet.summary: Starship filed a second suit challenging Gwinnett County?s adult-establishments ordinance after Georgia?s 2020 constitutional amendment created a waiver of sovereign immunity for certain declaratory and injunctive claims. The Supreme Court of Georgia held that the new constitutional waiver did apply to this kind of lawsuit, but Starship still lost. The Court affirmed dismissal because the suit was barred by res judicata. The reasoning was that Starship?s current constitutional attack arose from the same operative dispute over Gwinnett?s enforcement of the ordinance that was already at issue in the earlier litigation, and those constitutional objections either were or could have been raised there.

state-of-ga-v-fed-defender-program-inc-3.title: State of Ga. v. Fed. Defender Program, Inc.
state-of-ga-v-fed-defender-program-inc-3.summary: The Federal Defender Program sued Georgia officials, alleging that email exchanges about the scheduling of executions created a binding agreement that the State later broke. The issue was whether those emails amounted to a written contract sufficient to waive sovereign immunity and support injunctive relief. The Supreme Court of Georgia held that they did and affirmed the injunction. The court reasoned that the email correspondence set out enforceable terms in writing and therefore fell within Georgia?s constitutional waiver for written contracts. That meant the State could be sued for the alleged breach despite sovereign-immunity defenses.

state-of-ga-v-sistersong-women-of-color-.title: State of Ga. v. SisterSong Women of Color Reproductive Justice Collective
state-of-ga-v-sistersong-women-of-color-.summary: The State appealed after a trial court held key portions of Georgia?s 2019 LIFE Act void from the start because they conflicted with then-controlling federal abortion precedent later overruled by Dobbs. The issue before the Supreme Court of Georgia was narrow: whether a law can be deemed void ab initio simply because it would have been unconstitutional under federal decisions that no longer control. The Court said no, reversed, and remanded. It held that overruled federal precedent cannot permanently freeze a Georgia statute into nullity from the moment of enactment. The reasoning rejected the trial court?s void-ab-initio theory and sent the case back for the court to address, in the first instance, the plaintiffs? unresolved challenges under the Georgia Constitution, including privacy and due-process theories.

state-of-ga-v-sistersong.title: State of Ga. v. SisterSong Women of Color Reproductive Justice Collective
state-of-ga-v-sistersong.summary: The State appealed after a trial court held key portions of Georgia?s 2019 LIFE Act void from the start because they conflicted with then-controlling federal abortion precedent later overruled by Dobbs. The issue before the Supreme Court of Georgia was narrow: whether a law can be deemed void ab initio simply because it would have been unconstitutional under federal decisions that no longer control. The Court said no, reversed, and remanded. It held that overruled federal precedent cannot permanently freeze a Georgia statute into nullity from the moment of enactment. The reasoning rejected the trial court?s void-ab-initio theory and sent the case back for the court to address, in the first instance, the plaintiffs? unresolved challenges under the Georgia Constitution, including privacy and due-process theories.

state-v-arroyo-315-ga-582.title: State v. Arroyo
state-v-arroyo-315-ga-582.summary: Jose Arroyo was tried on drug-trafficking charges, and after the jury was sworn the trial court suppressed key evidence tied to a dog sniff and ultimately declared a mistrial. The State tried to appeal under OCGA 5-7-1, arguing that the suppression ruling fell within the statute authorizing certain prosecution appeals. The Supreme Court of Georgia held that the appeal was not authorized and vacated the Court of Appeals' merits decision. It ruled that the statutory right to appeal a suppression order does not extend to a ruling entered after jeopardy has attached and the trial has already begun. Because the order came midtrial, the State lacked a valid appellate vehicle, the Court of Appeals lacked jurisdiction, and the case had to be remanded with direction to dismiss the appeal.

state-v-brantley.title: State v. Brantley
state-v-brantley.summary: The State appealed a pretrial order excluding incriminating phone calls Barron Brantley made from the Fulton County jail while awaiting trial on murder and related charges arising from the killing of Alexis Crawford. The trial court had ruled that the district attorney's review of the recorded calls violated Brantley's privacy and equal-protection rights. The Supreme Court of Georgia reversed. It first held that the State was authorized to appeal the exclusion order, and it then held on the merits that Brantley had no reasonable expectation of privacy in non-attorney jail calls that began with an automated warning that they were monitored and recorded. The Court also rejected the equal-protection theory because an incarcerated defendant is not similarly situated to people outside jail, and the State has a rational basis for treating monitored jail calls differently. The governing rule is that recorded, non-privileged jail calls ordinarily carry no constitutionally protected expectation of privacy once the inmate has been warned they are being monitored, so exclusion cannot rest on a privacy or equal-protection theory of this kind.

state-v-dias.title: State v. Dias
state-v-dias.summary: Christine Dias moved to suppress evidence that she refused a warrantless blood test after a DUI arrest and persuaded the trial court that the governing statute, OCGA ? 40-6-392(d), was unconstitutional as applied to her. The Supreme Court of Georgia vacated that ruling and remanded. It held that the trial court relied on self-incrimination precedents dealing with breath tests, even though those cases did not decide the distinct question whether a defendant?s refusal of a blood test is inadmissible on search-and-seizure or due-process grounds. Because the trial court did not directly rule on that actual novel issue, and because nonconstitutional grounds for exclusion may still exist, the order could not stand. The Court left the underlying constitutional question open for proper consideration on remand.

state-v-harris-316-ga-272.title: State v. Harris
state-v-harris-316-ga-272.summary: In two murder prosecutions against Jada Harris, the trial court orally excluded evidence of prior incidents involving Harris and also suppressed a witness identification that followed a single-photograph display. The State appealed, raising the threshold question whether it could challenge those oral pretrial rulings before a written order issued, and the merits question whether the trial court had applied the right legal framework. The Supreme Court of Georgia held that appellate jurisdiction existed but vacated both rulings and remanded. It explained that the record was too undeveloped to support meaningful review because the trial court had not adequately set out the factual findings and legal reasoning needed to assess the exclusion of the prior-acts evidence or the identification evidence. The court's reasoning emphasized that suppression and admissibility decisions in serious criminal cases must be grounded in a clear analysis of the governing evidentiary and due-process rules, not left at the level of unexplained oral conclusions.

state-v-hillsman-368-ga-app-873.title: State v. Hillsman
state-v-hillsman-368-ga-app-873.summary: Dennis Hillsman pleaded guilty to armed robbery, but the trial court imposed a sentence below the statutory mandatory minimum even though the prosecutor never expressly agreed to that reduction. The issue on the State's appeal was whether the prosecutor's silence or failure to object at the plea hearing counted as the consent required for a below-minimum sentence. The Court of Appeals of Georgia answered no, vacated the sentence, and remanded for resentencing. It held that a departure from the mandatory minimum for armed robbery requires actual prosecutorial consent, not mere acquiescence or an undeveloped record. Because the State moved to correct the sentence and the record did not show the required agreement, the sentence was void and could not stand.

state-v-hylton.title: State v. Hylton
state-v-hylton.summary: Before trial on felony-murder and related charges, Shawn Hylton sought immunity from prosecution on the ground that he shot Lathan Davenport in self-defense after Davenport brutally attacked him and then advanced on him again minutes later. The State argued both that the trial court?s order was too thin to review and that the evidence did not justify deadly force. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed the grant of immunity. It held that explicit findings were not required and that the appellate court could infer the trial court?s factual and credibility determinations from the ruling itself. Viewing the evidence in the light most favorable to that ruling, the Court concluded there was sufficient evidence that Hylton reasonably believed deadly force was necessary to prevent death or great bodily injury, so immunity under OCGA ? 16-3-24.2 was properly granted.

state-v-jones-371-ga-app-445.title: State v. Jones
state-v-jones-371-ga-app-445.summary: The State appealed a suppression order in a DUI prosecution after the trial court excluded evidence tied to the stop, roadside investigation, and arrest of David Jones. The appellate issues included whether the officer had sufficient grounds to continue the detention and whether probable cause existed to arrest Jones for driving under the influence. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed part of the suppression ruling but vacated the portion addressing probable cause and remanded. It held that the trial court's order did not contain enough factual findings and legal analysis on the arrest question to permit meaningful appellate review. The court's reasoning followed the standard rule that suppression orders must show how the court resolved the evidence and applied the probable-cause framework, especially in DUI cases built on field observations and sobriety testing.

state-v-mckinney-366-ga-app-251.title: State v. McKinney
state-v-mckinney-366-ga-app-251.summary: The State charged Jerrion McKinney under Georgia?s Street Gang Terrorism and Prevention Act in connection with armed protests that followed the killing of Rayshard Brooks and the later death of Secoriea Turner. The issue was whether the indictment adequately alleged the required nexus between McKinney?s charged conduct and gang activity. The Court of Appeals of Georgia held that it did not and affirmed the grant of McKinney?s demurrer. The court reasoned that the State?s allegations did not move beyond broad protest-related conduct to plead the statutory gang connection the Act requires. Because that pleading defect went to the heart of the charge, the indictment could not stand as filed.

state-v-peppers.title: State v. Peppers
state-v-peppers.summary: A jury convicted Briana Peppers of second-degree murder and cruelty to children after the death of a child in her care, but the trial court later granted her a new trial on the ground that the evidence was legally insufficient. The issue on the State's appeal was whether a trial court may grant a post-verdict new trial by declaring the evidence insufficient, rather than using its separate authority to weigh the evidence as the thirteenth juror. The Court of Appeals of Georgia held that the trial court used the wrong legal path, vacated the order, and remanded. It explained that a true insufficiency ruling after conviction would amount to an acquittal and trigger double-jeopardy consequences, while a thirteenth-juror ruling involves a different discretionary assessment of the weight of the evidence. Because the order blurred those doctrines and rested on the wrong standard, it could not stand in its existing form.

state-v-wilson-315-ga-613.title: State v. Wilson
state-v-wilson-315-ga-613.summary: In a murder investigation, officers obtained a warrant authorizing a broad forensic search of cell phones that had already been seized, and the trial court suppressed the results of that search. The State appealed, arguing that the warrant was sufficiently tied to the homicide investigation and gave investigators lawful access to the phone contents. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed the suppression order. It held that the warrant lacked the required particularity and operated too much like a general warrant because it authorized sweeping review of digital data without a focused showing of why the specified phone contents would likely contain evidence of the crime. The court's reasoning stressed that cell-phone searches demand a concrete nexus and meaningful limits, not boilerplate authority to rummage through an entire digital life.

state-v-wood-367-ga-app-10.title: State v. Wood
state-v-wood-367-ga-app-10.summary: Police entered a hotel room without a warrant to respond to an apparent overdose emergency, helped stabilize the situation, and then later reentered and searched the room more extensively, leading to drug-trafficking charges against Wood. The State argued that the continued search was justified by the original emergency and, in any event, that the evidence would inevitably have been discovered. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed suppression. It held that the emergency-aid rationale ended once the immediate medical crisis was resolved and that the later reentry and search required a separate lawful basis the State did not have. The court also rejected inevitable discovery because the record did not show a truly independent and lawful investigatory path that would have uncovered the evidence anyway.

statham-v-quang.title: Statham v. Quang
statham-v-quang.summary: Jacqueline Statham alleged that she was injured during a hysterectomy because a supervised medical student misplaced a sponge stick, increasing the risk that the operating physicians would cut her rectal wall. The core issue was whether the physicians could be held vicariously liable for any negligence by the medical student. The Supreme Court of Georgia held that the defendants were not liable under OCGA ? 51-1-38 and could not be held liable under the borrowed-servant doctrine, because that doctrine functions as a defense within respondeat superior analysis rather than as an independent source of liability. But the Court reversed summary judgment for the physicians because the record created a jury question under ordinary agency principles: the evidence showed the doctors directly instructed and supervised the student?s physical conduct during the surgery, which could support a finding that the student was acting as their servant within the scope of their work.

steel-v-state.title: Steel v. State
steel-v-state.summary: Defense attorney Brian Steel was summarily held in contempt after refusing to reveal how he learned of an ex parte meeting during a high-profile criminal trial. The appeal asked whether the same trial judge could adjudicate and punish the contempt after becoming personally embroiled in the dispute and delaying the punishment rather than imposing it immediately to preserve courtroom order. The Supreme Court of Georgia reversed. It held that due process required the contempt matter to be heard by another judge because the circumstances showed sufficient personal embroilment and the delayed punishment removed the narrow justification for same-judge summary contempt. The court did not need to resolve the underlying privilege fight to decide the case; the contempt order failed because the procedure itself did not satisfy the neutrality required when judge and advocate become part of the controversy.

strong-v-state-2026-ga-lexis-76.title: Strong v. State
strong-v-state-2026-ga-lexis-76.summary: Aaron Strong was retried and again convicted of felony murder and related crimes after fatally stabbing his wife's adult son and seriously wounding another family member during a domestic confrontation at the family home. On appeal from the second trial, he challenged the exclusion of late-night text messages, the prosecutor's closing remarks about his refusal to reenact the stabbings on cross-examination, the trial court's refusal to charge mistake of fact, and his lawyer's performance in handling the text-message issue and references to Strong's first-trial testimony. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed across the board. It held that the evidentiary complaints did not warrant reversal, that the record did not support a mistake-of-fact instruction, and that Strong failed to show constitutionally deficient performance and prejudice under Strickland.
