The governing rule is that a defendant is not entitled to a jury charge or a new trial based on speculative theories untethered from the evidence, and ineffective-assistance claims fail without a concrete showing that counsel's alleged errors probably changed the outcome. Applying that framework, the Court concluded that none of the asserted trial errors undermined the verdict reached at the second trial.

suddarth-v-lounsbrough-369-ga-app-158.title: Suddarth v. Lounsbrough
suddarth-v-lounsbrough-369-ga-app-158.summary: A rural mail carrier slipped on leaves covering front steps at the Suddarth home while delivering a package after dark and sued the homeowners for premises liability. The interlocutory appeal turned on whether the homeowners had superior knowledge of the alleged hazard and whether the carrier could recover for a condition encountered in the course of the delivery. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed the denial of summary judgment. It held that Lounsbrough failed to show the homeowners knew of a hidden danger beyond what she herself could observe, which is fatal under Georgia premises-liability law. The court's reasoning followed the standard superior-knowledge rule: without proof that the landowners knew more than the invitee about the leaf-covered steps, the negligence claim could not proceed.

suresh-durga-inc-v-doe-369-ga-app-787.title: Suresh & Durga, Inc. v. Doe
suresh-durga-inc-v-doe-369-ga-app-787.summary: Jane Doe sued a motel after she was sexually assaulted and otherwise victimized on the property, alleging that the business ignored a dangerous pattern of criminal activity and operated the premises in a way that invited violent crime. The motel sought summary judgment on Doe's claims for premises liability, negligent hiring and supervision, nuisance, and attorney fees. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed in part, vacated in part, and remanded. It held that some of Doe's negligence theories survived because the record created fact questions about foreseeability, security conditions, and the motel's knowledge of criminal risks on the premises. But the court required additional work on other portions of the order, making clear that each theory had to be analyzed under its own governing rules rather than allowed to rise or fall as one undifferentiated negligence claim.

tallington-v-state.title: Tallington v. State
tags: Commercial Sexual Exploitation of a Minor
tallington-v-state.summary: Christopher Tallington was convicted of trafficking a minor for sexual servitude and several related sex offenses after evidence showed that he recruited a 14-year-old runaway into motel-based prostitution, took explicit photos of her, monitored her commercial sex encounters, and kept the proceeds. On appeal, he challenged both the sufficiency of the evidence and trial counsel?s performance, including counsel?s failure to pursue suppression arguments aimed at a phone tied to the Backpage ads. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that the victim?s testimony was sufficiently corroborated and that the surrounding digital, photographic, and physical evidence strongly supported the convictions. It also concluded that Tallington failed to show prejudice from counsel?s strategic choices because the proposed suppression theories, including his attack on the phone warrant, would not likely have succeeded.

tatum-v-state-367-ga-app-439.title: Tatum v. State
tatum-v-state-367-ga-app-439.summary: Mark Tatum was convicted after an officer seized his phone during a peeping-Tom investigation, viewed part of its contents without a warrant, and then obtained a search warrant that led to the phone evidence used at trial. Tatum argued that the later warrant was tainted because the affidavit and investigative path were infected by the initial warrantless search. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed the denial of suppression. It assumed without deciding that the officer's initial viewing of the phone was unlawful but held that the evidence was still admissible under the independent source doctrine because the warrant affidavit contained enough untainted information to establish probable cause. On that view, the lawful warrant broke the chain between the possible initial illegality and the phone evidence admitted at trial.

tatum-v-state.title: Tatum v. State
tatum-v-state.summary: After Mark Tatum secretly recorded a neighbor through her window, police seized his phone, viewed part of it without a warrant, and then used information from the investigation to secure a warrant for a fuller search. The question on certiorari was whether the independent source doctrine could save the later warrant search without examining whether the unlawful phone viewing prompted the decision to seek the warrant in the first place. The Supreme Court of Georgia said no, vacated the Court of Appeals decision, and remanded. It held that the doctrine requires more than a cleaned-up probable-cause affidavit; courts must also determine whether officers would have sought the warrant absent the initial illegal search. Because that causal inquiry had not been properly addressed, the suppression analysis was incomplete.

thomas-v-henry-county-water-auth-367-ga-.title: Thomas v. Henry County Water Auth.
thomas-v-henry-county-water-auth-367-ga-.summary: Rickey Thomas alleged that a Henry County Water Authority employee negligently crashed a work truck into his vehicle, causing catastrophic injuries, and he and his wife sued both the authority and the employee. The defendants argued that the suit failed because the Thomases had not complied with the county ante litem statute and because the employee was immune from suit. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed the dismissal. It held that the complaint could go forward because the Water Authority was not entitled to dismissal on the ante litem theory advanced and the employee was not shown to be immune at the pleading stage on the allegations presented. The reasoning turned on the distinction between counties and other governmental entities and on the limited scope of dismissal when the complaint alleges ordinary negligence in a vehicle collision.

thomas-v-state-366-ga-app-738.title: Thomas v. State
thomas-v-state-366-ga-app-738.summary: Tommy Lamar Thomas filed a special demurrer to weapon counts charging him with possessing a sawed-off rifle and a sawed-off shotgun, arguing that the indictment was too vague to tell him what specific firearms he had to defend against. The issue was whether those counts gave constitutionally adequate notice or instead used descriptions so generic that Thomas could not prepare a defense or plead former jeopardy in any later prosecution. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed the denial of the special demurrer. It held that the challenged counts lacked sufficient detail about the weapons and therefore failed to satisfy the notice function required of a criminal indictment when timely attacked by special demurrer. The court's reasoning underscored that, when the State chooses to prosecute weapon-possession counts tied to particular firearms, it must describe them with enough specificity to identify the alleged offenses.

torres-toledo-v-state-366-ga-app-526.title: Torres-Toledo v. State
torres-toledo-v-state-366-ga-app-526.summary: Javier Torres-Toledo pleaded guilty to armed robbery and aggravated assault with a deadly weapon and later argued that the State had breached an earlier plea agreement connected to another case. The Court of Appeals of Georgia rejected his claim that an enforceable plea agreement had been proved on this record, but it identified a separate sentencing problem. It held that the aggravated-assault conviction should have merged into the armed-robbery conviction under the facts of the plea, so both punishments could not stand independently. The judgment was therefore affirmed in part, vacated in part, and remanded for resentencing, with the court making clear that the merger error, not the alleged plea bargain breach, drove the relief.

trabue-v-hanson.title: Trabue v. Hanson
trabue-v-hanson.summary: After years of malpractice litigation, Keith Trabue filed a separate abusive-litigation suit against opposing attorneys David Hanson and Michael Flint, accusing them of filing repetitive post-judgment attacks to delay payment of a large verdict. Flint responded with an anti-SLAPP motion, and Hanson sought fees under OCGA ? 9-15-14; Trabue then voluntarily dismissed the abusive-litigation complaint without prejudice. The trial court still awarded fees to both attorneys, and the Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that Flint remained the prevailing moving party under the anti-SLAPP statute even after the voluntary dismissal because the suit was legally defective when challenged, and that Hanson was entitled to fees because the abusive-litigation action was concededly premature and therefore lacked any justiciable basis. The court?s reasoning emphasized that voluntary dismissal does not erase fee exposure when the suit should not have been filed in the first place.

tussahaw-reserves-llc-v-butts-cnty.title: Tussahaw Reserves, LLC v. Butts Cnty.
tussahaw-reserves-llc-v-butts-cnty.summary: Tussahaw challenged Butts County?s denial of a rezoning request for a rock quarry through a combined certiorari and declaratory-judgment action that named the County as defendant and also listed the Board and its members as respondents-in-certiorari. After this Court?s later sovereign-immunity precedent, the County argued the entire case had to be dismissed because the complaint did not comply with the Georgia Constitution?s requirement that such actions be brought exclusively against the county in the county?s name. The Supreme Court of Georgia vacated the dismissal and remanded. It held that any failure to satisfy Paragraph V(b)(2)?s naming rule is procedural, not jurisdictional, and therefore does not strip the trial court of power to act or prevent it from dropping unnecessary parties under OCGA ? 9-11-21. Because that procedural path could resolve the case, the Court declined to decide the harder constitutional questions about how to characterize the respondents-in-certiorari or whether courts should look through the caption to the substance of the pleading.

united-states-v-garza-2023-u-s-app-lexis.title: United States v. Garza
united-states-v-garza-2023-u-s-app-lexis.summary: Justin Garza pleaded guilty to multiple methamphetamine-trafficking counts and sought safety-valve relief to avoid the ten-year mandatory minimum sentence. The issue was whether he had truthfully provided the government with all the information he had about the offense, including the details of his suppliers and the larger conspiracy. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed the district court's finding that he was ineligible. It held that the record supported the conclusion that Garza had not fully disclosed everything he knew, which meant he failed the statutory truth-telling requirement for safety-valve relief. Because that requirement is mandatory, the district court properly left the ten-year floor in place.

united-states-v-martin-2023-u-s-app-lexi.title: United States v. Martin
united-states-v-martin-2023-u-s-app-lexi.summary: Michael Martin received a 12-month-and-one-day sentence after the district court revoked his supervised release, and he argued on appeal that the sentence was too harsh because the court did not give enough weight to his depression, stress, and claimed use of marijuana as self-medication. The issue was whether the revocation sentence was substantively unreasonable under the Section 3553(a) framework. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed. It held that Martin failed to show the district court abused its broad sentencing discretion, especially given the deference appellate courts owe to revocation sentences and the court's consideration of the record as a whole. The panel's reasoning emphasized that a sentencing judge need not accept a defendant's preferred mitigation narrative or explicitly credit every explanation for repeated violations.

united-states-v-nicholl-2023-u-s-app-lex.title: United States v. Nicholl
united-states-v-nicholl-2023-u-s-app-lex.summary: Atlanta officers arrested Jamal Nicholl for laying drag after he repeatedly burned his tires in a crowded entertainment district, and a search incident to arrest uncovered a firearm that led to a federal felon-in-possession charge. Nicholl argued that the gun should have been suppressed because the officers lacked probable cause to arrest him for the traffic offense. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed the denial of suppression and the conviction. It held that the officers had probable cause to believe Nicholl committed the Georgia offense based on their observations of the tire burnouts and smoke in traffic. Because the arrest was lawful, the search of Nicholl's person and the seizure of the gun were lawful as well.

united-states-v-perez-quevedo-2022-u-s-a.title: United States v. Perez-Quevedo
united-states-v-perez-quevedo-2022-u-s-a.summary: Perez-Quevedo challenged his sentence after participating in a large-scale cocaine-smuggling scheme that used semi-submersible vessels to move drugs from Colombia toward Mexico for eventual distribution into the United States. The issue was whether the district court should have granted him a minor-role reduction under USSG ? 3B1.2. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed. It held that the sentencing court did not clearly err in refusing the reduction because Perez-Quevedo?s role, measured against the conduct for which he was held accountable and against the structure of the trafficking venture, was not shown to be substantially less culpable than the relevant participants. The court?s reasoning followed the ordinary minor-role framework rather than treating his status in a broader cartel pipeline as enough by itself to lower the sentence.

united-states-v-tia-deyon-pugh-90-f-4th-.title: United States v. Tia Deyon Pugh
united-states-v-tia-deyon-pugh-90-f-4th-.summary: Tia Pugh was convicted under 18 USC 231(a)(3) after she smashed the window of a police car during a protest that had moved toward an interstate ramp, and she challenged the statute itself on multiple constitutional grounds. The appeal asked whether the civil-disorder statute exceeded Congress's Commerce Clause power, swept too broadly under the First Amendment, imposed a content-based speech restriction, or was unconstitutionally vague. The Eleventh Circuit rejected all four theories and affirmed. It held that the statute validly reaches conduct that impedes law enforcement during a civil disorder affecting interstate commerce, targets obstructive conduct rather than protected expression, and gives fair notice of what it forbids. The court's reasoning treated the case as a regulation of violent interference with police activity, not a criminalization of protest as such.

upshaw-v-state-323-ga-257.title: Upshaw v. State
upshaw-v-state-323-ga-257.summary: Three defendants appealed convictions arising from a retaliatory shooting in Muscogee County that left two victims dead and two others wounded after the victims drove through an apartment complex armed and masked. The appeals raised overlapping challenges to Gang Act evidence and the sufficiency of the proof, while individual defendants also attacked the rejection of a justification theory, the exclusion of social-media evidence tied to the victims, and the admission of a Scarface meme. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed all convictions and sentences. It held that the State's proof was sufficient to support the malice-murder and gang-related counts, that the prior-offense evidence was properly admitted to prove criminal gang activity, and that the remaining evidentiary complaints were either harmless or failed under the applicable standard of review. The governing rule is that gang prosecutions may rely on prior criminal conduct when the statute permits it and that appellate courts will not disturb murder verdicts supported by evidence of a coordinated armed ambush merely because defendants offer alternate explanations. The Court concluded that none of the asserted evidentiary or constitutional errors justified a new trial.

ussery-v-state-366-ga-app-379.title: Ussery v. State
ussery-v-state-366-ga-app-379.summary: Columbus Ussery challenged his per se DUI conviction by arguing that the State failed to prove the person who drew his blood was qualified under OCGA 40-6-392, which would have required exclusion of the blood-test results. He also argued that, without the blood evidence, the remaining proof could not sustain the conviction. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that the State laid an adequate statutory foundation for the blood draw and that the trial court did not abuse its discretion in admitting the test results. Once that evidence properly came in, the proof was sufficient to support the per se DUI conviction.

venticinque-v-lair.title: Venticinque v. Lair
venticinque-v-lair.summary: Amber Lair sought equitable-caregiver status and custody rights over the biological child of her former partner, Krystle Venticinque, after the couple separated and Venticinque cut off contact. The trial court granted Lair equitable-caregiver status and later awarded her joint legal custody and primary physical custody. The issue on appeal was whether the trial court applied the correct standard under OCGA ? 19-7-3.1 and whether the Equitable Caregiver Statute is constitutional. The Supreme Court of Georgia vacated both the equitable-caregiver ruling and the custody order. It held that the trial court improperly placed the burden on Venticinque to show the child would be harmed if Lair?s petition were granted, when the statute instead requires the petitioner to prove by clear and convincing evidence that the child will suffer physical harm or long-term emotional harm without continuing the relationship and that continued contact is in the child?s best interest. Because that threshold question must be decided correctly first, the Court did not reach the broader constitutional challenge.

voterga-v-state-of-georgia-368-ga-app-11.title: Voterga v. State of Georgia
voterga-v-state-of-georgia-368-ga-app-11.summary: VoterGA and an individual elector sued the State of Georgia, attacking the legality of Georgia's electronic voting system and seeking declaratory and injunctive relief over the use of ballot-marking devices. The issue on appeal was whether the petition stated viable claims that could proceed and whether declaratory judgment was the right procedural vehicle for the asserted election-integrity injuries. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed dismissal. It held that the petitioners failed to state a claim upon which relief could be granted against the State on the allegations pleaded. The court's reasoning emphasized that broad objections to the voting system and its certification did not, in the form presented, translate into a legally sufficient declaratory-judgment action.

walsh-v-bowen-367-ga-app-208.title: Walsh v. Bowen
walsh-v-bowen-367-ga-app-208.summary: After Rick Walsh died, a dispute arose over whether he had effectively given funds to educational savings accounts owned by his wife for her granddaughters, or whether the money remained part of his estate. The executor argued that no valid gift occurred because the funds were never delivered during Walsh's lifetime, while the opposing side pointed to his instructions and steps to transfer control of the money. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed the grant of partial summary judgment to the executor. It held that a jury could find constructive delivery because there was evidence Walsh took actions showing he renounced dominion over the funds and intended to transfer that dominion to his wife through the 529 accounts. That factual question barred summary judgment.

watts-v-joseph-374-ga-app-367.title: Watts v. Joseph
watts-v-joseph-374-ga-app-367.summary: Michael Joseph sued the wrong driver after a multi-vehicle collision and later moved to add Tyler Watts after the limitation period had run, arguing that the amended complaint related back because Watts had enough notice of the litigation. The trial court denied Watts's dispositive motion, and Watts took an interlocutory appeal. The issue was whether pre-suit and insurer correspondence gave Watts the notice required by OCGA 9-11-15 (c) for relation back after Joseph initially sued the vehicle owner instead of the actual driver. The Court of Appeals of Georgia held that the notice showing was insufficient and reversed. It reasoned that an October 2018 letter merely informed Watts of a claim and investigation before any suit existed, and there was no competent evidence that Watts actually received later notice of the filed action before the statute expired. Without timely notice of the institution of the action, the amendment could not relate back, so the claims against Watts were time-barred.

weaver-v-millsaps-370-ga-app-513.title: Weaver v. Millsaps
weaver-v-millsaps-370-ga-app-513.summary: After Michael Weaver encouraged others to post negative Google reviews of Valerie Millsaps's frame shop, Millsaps responded publicly by calling him a Neo-Nazi, a felon, and someone who had threatened others. Weaver sued for libel, and Millsaps moved to dismiss under Georgia's anti-SLAPP statute. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed the dismissal. It held that Millsaps's statements fell within protected speech covered by the anti-SLAPP framework and that Weaver failed to show a probability of prevailing on his defamation claim. The court's reasoning reflects the statute's purpose: to shut down speech-targeting litigation early when the challenged statements arise from protected public expression and the plaintiff cannot make a strong merits showing.

wells-v-khan-shell-llc.title: Wells v. Khan Shell, LLC
wells-v-khan-shell-llc.summary: Tara Wells tripped in a gas-station parking lot after leaving a funeral, later pointing to holes and uneven concrete seams as the cause of her fall. The question was whether Khan Shell had superior knowledge of a hazardous condition or whether the condition was an open and obvious static defect that Wells could have avoided by exercising ordinary care. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed summary judgment for the station owner. It held that the uneven pavement was a static condition, that nothing obstructed Wells?s view, and that Wells herself admitted the condition was easy to see once she looked. Because Georgia premises-liability law does not allow recovery where the invitee had equal knowledge of an open and obvious condition, her claim failed.

whitaker-farms-llc-v-fitzgerald-fruit-fa.title: Whitaker Farms, LLC v. Fitzgerald Fruit Farms, LLC
whitaker-farms-llc-v-fitzgerald-fruit-fa.summary: After Fitzgerald Fruit Farms won compensatory damages against Whitaker Farms for locking it out of leased orchard property, a second trial produced a punitive-damages award. The Supreme Court of Georgia took up whether statements made during settlement negotiations were properly admitted at that punitive-damages trial under Georgia Rule of Evidence 408. It held that they were not, vacated the Court of Appeals decision, and remanded. The court ruled that the statements were inadmissible settlement-negotiation evidence under the rule and that the lower appellate court had erred in approving their use. The remaining question on remand was whether that evidentiary error was harmful enough to require a new trial on punitive damages.

white-v-mcgouirk-370-ga-app-318.title: White v. McGouirk
white-v-mcgouirk-370-ga-app-318.summary: Amy McGouirk and her husband won more than $10 million in a medical-malpractice trial after alleging that Dr. Tamica White transected Amy's common bile duct during gallbladder surgery. On appeal, the defense argued that plaintiffs' counsel made improper closing remarks that warranted a mistrial or new trial and that the verdict was excessive. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that the trial court did not commit reversible error in managing the challenged closing argument and did not abuse its discretion by leaving the verdict intact. The court's reasoning emphasized the deference owed to a jury verdict approved by the trial court and the demanding standard for overturning a damages award as excessive.

williams-v-harvey-366-ga-app-395.title: Williams v. Harvey
williams-v-harvey-366-ga-app-395.summary: The Sons of Confederate Veterans sued the City of Brunswick after the City voted to remove a Confederate monument, claiming the removal violated Georgia's monument-protection statute and seeking damages and injunctive relief. The issue was whether the organization had standing to sue based on its members' use of the monument, past restoration spending, and claimed interests in the memorial. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed dismissal. It held that the group failed to allege the kind of concrete and particularized injury necessary to establish standing. The court's reasoning tracked prior monument litigation and made clear that ideological interest, historical affiliation, and generalized displeasure with removal do not by themselves open the courthouse door.

williams-v-kasulka-properties-lp-370-ga-.title: Williams v. Kasulka Properties, LP
williams-v-kasulka-properties-lp-370-ga-.summary: Damario Williams sued after he was seriously injured in a fight with a nightclub security guard, naming not just the club operators but also the landlord and affiliated property-management entities. The appeal asked whether those defendants could be liable despite the lease placing repair and control obligations on the tenant. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed summary judgment for the landlord and managers. It held that the landlord was an out-of-possession owner shielded by Georgia law and that the property-management entities had not violated any duty owed to Williams on this record. The court's reasoning turned on possession and control: without control over the nightclub's day-to-day operations or a breached duty tied to the assault, those defendants could not be held liable.

williams-v-state-315-ga-490.title: Williams v. State
williams-v-state-315-ga-490.summary: Antonio Williams was convicted of felony murder and a firearm offense after a marijuana deal turned into a retaliatory killing, and he challenged both the admission of cell-site location evidence and a jury charge on the testimony of a single witness. The issues were whether the CSLI warrant was an impermissible general warrant and whether the jury should have been instructed more specifically on accomplice corroboration. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed. It held that Williams failed to establish plain error as to the CSLI evidence and likewise failed to show reversible error in the jury instructions, particularly under the demanding plain-error standard. The court's reasoning reflects a familiar appellate rule: when objections are not preserved, a defendant must show a clear and outcome-determinative mistake, and Williams did not.

wilson-v-state.title: Wilson v. State
wilson-v-state.summary: Kalone Wilson was convicted of aggravated assault, family-violence battery, and criminal trespass after attacking his former partner at a gas station, and he argued that the State was allowed to use an expert it had not timely disclosed in discovery. He also argued that the trial court used the wrong legal standard in qualifying that expert. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that the trial court did not commit reversible error in admitting the testimony or in qualifying the witness under the governing evidentiary standard. The court's reasoning shows substantial deference to the trial court's management of expert evidence and found no prejudice sufficient to justify a new trial.

wise-bus-forms-inc-v-forsyth-county.title: Wise Bus. Forms, Inc. v. Forsyth County
wise-bus-forms-inc-v-forsyth-county.summary: Wise Business Forms sued Forsyth County, alleging that drainage work connected to a road-widening project created a permanent nuisance that repeatedly damaged its property. The certiorari issue was when an inverse-condemnation claim by permanent nuisance accrues for purposes of Georgia's four-year statute of limitation. The Supreme Court of Georgia reversed the dismissal and remanded. It held that the Court of Appeals applied the accrual analysis too rigidly and failed to construe the complaint in Wise's favor, especially where the alleged harms unfolded over time and were not necessarily all immediately observable when the project was completed. The court's reasoning clarifies that permanent-nuisance accrual turns on when the relevant harm becomes reasonably ascertainable, not simply on the date the public work was finished.

wood-v-state.title: Wood v. State
wood-v-state.summary: Tabitha Wood admitted killing her fiance and defended the case as self-defense, presenting evidence of his long history of abuse and expert testimony that she suffered from battered person syndrome. On appeal, she argued that the trial court should also have allowed testimony about the victim's prior violence toward other women under Rule 405 and that excluding that evidence violated her right to present a complete defense. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed. It held that specific acts of a victim's violence toward third parties were not admissible under Rule 405(b) because the victim's violent character is not an essential element of self-defense. The court also concluded that Wood had not shown a clear constitutional violation from the trial court's application of ordinary evidentiary rules.

wood-v-state-366-ga-app-305.title: Wood v. State
wood-v-state-366-ga-app-305.summary: Brandon Wood was convicted of aggravated assault after striking another man in the head with a baseball bat during a confrontation at an apartment complex. He argued on appeal that trial counsel was ineffective and that the jury should have been charged on simple assault as a lesser offense. The Court of Appeals of Georgia affirmed. It held that Wood failed to establish ineffective assistance under the governing performance-and-prejudice standard and that the evidence did not require a simple-assault instruction. The court's reasoning reflects the usual rule that a lesser-offense charge is not required when the evidence points to the completed greater assault or no crime at all.

wood-v-state-316-ga-811.title: Wood v. State
wood-v-state-316-ga-811.summary: Bobby Wood was convicted of felony murder after shooting Aaron Skinner and then claiming Skinner had come at him with what looked like a weapon. On appeal, Wood challenged limits on cross-examining the State's expert about the victim's prior trespass arrest, the denial of post-trial access to certain physical evidence, counsel's handling of redirect examination, and cumulative error. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed. It held that none of the individual rulings amounted to reversible error and that the ineffective-assistance claim failed because Wood could not show both deficient performance and resulting prejudice. With no underlying reversible errors, the cumulative-error theory also went nowhere.

woodruff-v-jones-366-ga-app-393.title: Woodruff v. Jones
woodruff-v-jones-366-ga-app-393.summary: Diamond Woodruff's personal-injury suit was dismissed after the trial court concluded she failed to move for substitution within 180 days after a suggestion of the defendant's death was filed. The issue was whether that 180-day clock had ever started to run when the record showed no personal service of the suggestion of death on the nonparty representative of the decedent's estate. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed. It held that the statutory period does not begin until that required service occurs and is reflected in the record. Because the necessary service was missing, dismissal under OCGA 9-11-25 was premature.

wright-v-state-315-ga-459.title: Wright v. State
wright-v-state-315-ga-459.summary: Walter Wright was convicted of felony murder after a woman died from a gunshot wound during an encounter inside her home, and he argued that the evidence was insufficient and that the trial court gave an improper good-character instruction. He also claimed trial counsel was ineffective for failing to object to that charge. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed. It held that the evidence supported the conviction, that the character instruction tracked the approved pattern charge and was not erroneous, and that counsel was not deficient for declining to raise a meritless objection. The court's reasoning left no room for either plain instructional error or a derivative ineffective-assistance claim.

york-v-moore-367-ga-app-152.title: York v. Moore
york-v-moore-367-ga-app-152.summary: Homeowner Margaret Moore sued general contractor Tommy York after a fire broke out during renovations, allegedly because painting materials were left in a dangerous pile by a subcontractor. The issue was whether York could be held liable under an exception to the general rule that a principal is not responsible for an independent contractor's torts. The Court of Appeals of Georgia reversed the denial of summary judgment to York. It held that Moore failed to produce evidence of an express contract triggering the statutory exception she relied on under OCGA 51-2-5(3). Without proof of that exception, the ordinary non-liability rule for a general contractor and an independent contractor controlled.
